data.title: Heathrow to fine drivers £80 for overly long farewells
data.description:
People who take too long to bid farewell to loved ones at Heathrow face an £80 fine for overstaying their welcome.

A new 10-minute limit for unloading bags and wishing passengers a safe journey at Britain’s busiest airport will leave no time for drawn-out goodbyes.

The cap will be introduced for drivers at Heathrow’s forecourts from January as part of a shake-up that will push drop-off charges up from £6 to £7 in an inflation-busting 17pc increase.

Unlike most other major airports in the UK, Heathrow has not previously sought to limit the time people can spend dropping off friends, family and business colleagues.

However, hubs such as Gatwick and Luton allow over-stayers to pay by the minute rather than hitting them with a hefty fine.

Clive Wratten, the chief executive of the Business Travel Association, a lobby group, said the higher fees and strict time limit risk punishing passengers unnecessarily.

He said: “There’s next to no justification for this. These charges do nothing to improve the passenger experience or reliability of access to Heathrow. All it does is raise costs.”

Heathrow claimed that the 10-minute maximum stay, to be introduced across all of its terminal forecourts, would ease congestion and improve traffic flows.

It said: “This will enhance the customer experience for those using the terminal drop off as it is intended.”

It said 95pc of drop-offs take less than 10 minutes and that the time limit is expected to have a “minimal impact”.

A spokesman added: “Most people just drop off and go, they don’t hang around. It’s very busy, and we need to move people on. There just isn’t space in those terminal forecourts.”

One response to the scheme, however, could be for drivers who need more than 10 minutes to circle round and return to the drop-off area, accruing a £14 fee rather than the £80 fine, potentially adding to congestion.

Heathrow said the £7 basic charge – also backed up by an £80 fine for non-payment, reduced to £40 if settled within 14 days – brings it into line with other airports and is aimed partly at encouraging “options for travel that have a lower environmental impact”.

While Gatwick already charges £7 for the first 10 minutes on its forecourts, people can stay for longer at a cost of £1 per minute, up to a maximum of 30 minutes and £27.

Luton operates a similar system, charging £7 for 10 minutes and £1 per minute thereafter, again with a maximum stay of 30 minutes.

Manchester and Stansted airports have somewhat lower basic charges but a flat £25 rate for people who extend their time in the drop-off areas.

Heathrow said blue-badge holders will be exempt from the maximum stay limit and will still be able to use drop-off zones for free, so long as they register their trip.

data.title: The Shard’s brickmaker collapses into administration
data.description:
The brickmaker that supplied The Shard has collapsed into administration amid a protracted slowdown in construction.

York Handmade Brick filed a notice of intention to appoint administrators on Friday, according to court records, and is represented by Addleshaw Goddard.

The manufacturer supplied 90,000 bespoke bricks to The Shard, which opened in 2013. The 87-storey skyscraper is predominantly glass and steel but has bricks on its first few floors, including a dramatic brickwork façade.

York bricks have also been used at London Bridge railway station, with 100,000 provided for the station’s overhaul ahead of its reopening in 2017.

Other projects it has been involved in include Magdalene College Library in Cambridge, which won the Royal Institute of British Architects’s Stirling Prize in 2022.

The company also supplied the bricks for the restoration of St Albans Cathedral in Hertfordshire, the oldest site of continuous Christian worship in Britain, the Westgate Oxford shopping centre redevelopment and York Racecourse.

Based at Alne, near Easingwold, the brickmaker has been running since 1988. It was founded by David Armitage, a fifth-generation brickmaker, and remains a family business. Guy Armitage, David’s son, is now the company’s managing director.

The company’s collapse into administration comes at a torrid time for construction suppliers and builders, as rising costs and a downturn in building heap pressure on the industry.

In a trading update on Tuesday, fellow brick manufacturer Michelmersh warned of a “notable slowdown” in the industry during the final quarter of the year.

Michelmersh said activity has been hit by a “challenging macro-economic outlook and the uncertainty of UK Budget policy announcements, which have adversely impacted both consumer sentiment and investment decision making”.

Builders were left disappointed by last week’s Budget, including Richard Beresford, chief executive of the National Federation of Builders, who said in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s statement that the “industry will remain in the mud”.

“Death by a thousand taxes has already killed off many businesses, with insolvency rates still high within the construction industry,” he said.

The construction sector shrank at its fastest pace in more than five years in October, according to the S&P Global UK Construction PMI index.

Brick makers are also among the industries struggling with high energy costs, and relief for manufacturers has been pushed back to 2027.

York Handmade Brick were contacted for comment, while Addleshaw Goddard declined to comment.

data.title: Qatar dumps £270m stake in Sainsbury’s
data.description:
Qatar’s sovereign wealth fund has dumped a £270m stake in Sainsbury’s, losing its crown as the supermarket’s largest shareholder.

On Tuesday, the Qatar Investment Authority (QIA) launched a surprise stock sell-down, offering nearly 84 million shares to other large shareholders in a deal led by JP Morgan.

The move – announced after the markets closed – will cut QIA’s stake in Sainsbury’s from 10.5pc to just under 7pc.

It means QIA will lose its status as the supermarket chain’s largest shareholder, having sat at the top of the register since it acquired shares in the group in 2007.

News of the change sparked a slump in Sainsbury’s share price after the markets opened on Wednesday, with the retailer tumbling by almost 4pc. It made Sainsbury’s the biggest faller on the FTSE 100, with shares in the supermarket trading at their lowest level since early September.

Royal Mail owner Daniel Kretinsky, through his Luxembourg-based Vesa Equity Investments, will become the top Sainsbury’s shareholder, with a 10.3pc holding.

Mr Kretinsky’s Vesa originally acquired its Sainsbury’s shares by purchasing them from QIA, buying a 7pc stake in the company when QIA’s holding was closer to 25pc.

BlackRock and Vanguard have stakes of about 5.7pc and 5.6pc, respectively, in Sainsbury’s.

Danish billionaire Anders Holch Povlsen’s Bestway Group, one of the UK’s biggest grocery wholesalers and owner of Well Pharmacy, also has a 5.3pc stake in the supermarket.

QIA has gradually been selling its shares, trimming its 25pc stake down to 10pc and now 7pc with the latest sale.

Last October, it sold a £306m stake in Sainsbury’s – reducing its holding by 5pc – after the supermarket’s chief executive, Simon Roberts, warned that uncertainty regarding Rachel Reeves’s Budget would hit sales.

The QIA sale comes after Sainsbury’s became Britain’s most shorted stock by hedge funds earlier this year. Investors had bet against 7pc of the company’s shares amid fears the group would lose out in a supermarket price war.

Hedge funds have since trimmed their bets against the group to account for about 6pc of shares.

The Telegraph also disclosed earlier this year that a Chinese takeover of Argos was planned after Sainsbury’s decided to sell the chain.

But discussions were terminated just 24 hours after confirming it planned to sell the retailer, a move that cast doubt on Mr Roberts’ strategy.

The supermarket chain bought Argos for £1.1bn nine years ago but valued it at just £344m in recent accounts.

Rivals Asda and Aldi have continued to put pressure on the supermarket with their more competitive pricing.

However, Sainsbury’s reported higher-than-expected underlying profit in its most recent quarter of just over £1bn. Shares in the company have risen by almost 14pc in the past six months.

Sainsbury’s was contacted for comment.

data.title: To restore Britain, start with the rail dining car
data.description:
Among stiff competition, few experiences more totally obliterate any lingering faith in Britain today than the average train journey. Penned like cattle into stiff plastic seats, stifled by delays and cancellations, bombarded by youths playing music out loud and preyed upon by conductors yearning to triple charge you for what you thought was the right ticket, the average railway journey is an express train into misery.

Yet amid this litany of woe, there is the worst offender. I’ve had some depressing meals in my time, from school dinner slop to peculiar post-club kebabs. But no culinary catastrophe comes close to the miserable pickings offered by your average rail buffet car. Remembering that half of what is mislabelled a menu is missing at any one time, one is usually offered a bleak selection of stale sandwiches, tepid bacon rolls, unimaginative crisps and depressing paninis. Before boarding a long-distance train today, I always make a packed lunch.

I wasn’t always so dour. I was a train-mad tot, raised between Thomas the Tank Engine, toy tracks and weekend Tube trips. As I grew older, I wept over pictures of disused stations, put railway posters up with my Margaret Thatcher and Jenna Coleman portraits, and re-watched John Betjeman’s Metro-Land with a fanaticism that would put many a jihadi to shame. But the fantasy has died as my professional obligations have made train travel a daily dread.

It shouldn’t have to be like this. We did once know how to make the experience enjoyable. Travelling by rail through Britain should be joyous. Even if one has to stop off at a crime against architecture grotesquely mislabelled as a station, one yearns to see our country’s magnificence while hurtling between destinations in far greater comfort than a motorway slog. An obvious ground zero for rail restorationism is the dining car.

Anyone who has seen North by Northwest or From Russia with Love can attest to the inherent charm of an old-fashioned dining car. Who wouldn’t want a long-distance railway journey enhanced by tucking in to a three-course meal with a decent claret or three? Who wouldn’t rather enjoy a cocktail as the country passes by than a lukewarm lager or oddly frosty coffee? Luxury train journeys with dining experiences – like the Northern Belle, branded “Britain’s poshest train”, and available for booking for Christmas dinner – are doing great business. Why not bring this back to the masses?

There is a 150-odd year tradition of doing so. The Pullman dining car is synonymous with luxury. Originally built by the Pullman Company for dining, sleeping and lounge use, the term still applies to a few remaining high-end train dining services. But the number of them has shrunk precipitously. When the railways were privatised in 1995 almost 250 trains a day offered a dining car. Within two decades, that had shrunk to only four. Privatisation saw passenger numbers double and investment increase nine-old, but at the cost of making journeys soulless.

With Labour’s renationalisation push, there is a chance for a reset. Yet the first returns of renewed state-control are predictably unpromising, with delays and cancellations on South Western Railway rising by 50 per cent since nationalisation. Labour’s technocrats lack the vision required to restore our railways. Which is why the cause of the dining car should be taken up by a politician with a far better appreciation of a long lunch’s merits: Nigel Farage.

The Reform leader may prefer private helicopter to rail. But with his newfound penchant for nationalisation, Farage is already fishing in this pool. What could be more appropriate than pledging to put a Boisdale’s on every inter-city service? Back in 2010, Ukip pledged to “make the Circle line a circle again”. That is the spirit in which Farage shall save our trains.

William Atkinson is assistant content editor at The Spectator
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Eurostar needs more competition than just Branson’s Virgin
data.description:
There will be some cabin crew in bright red uniforms. There will probably be some loud music and plenty of dry ice. And Sir Richard Branson could almost certainly show up in a rocket, a balloon or perhaps even a submarine, pulling some kind of stunt or other.

We can expect plenty of glitzy razzamatazz when the first Virgin train heads out of London to the Continent in a few years’ time, breaking Eurostar’s monopoly of rail travel through the Channel Tunnel.

And yet we should not stop there. There should be lots of operators competing on that route – not just two.

After a long struggle, Virgin last week finally won its battle to run trains to the rest of Europe after securing approval from the Office of Rail and Road.

It will still need to get safety approvals from regulators in both the UK and the European Union, negotiate platform space and work out a deal with Eurostar. But it hopes to have a service to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam up and running by the end of the decade.

For the first time in the three decades since the tunnel linking the UK to the rest of Europe was opened, passengers will finally be able to choose which company they want to travel with.

And at least one rail route in the UK will get some much-needed competition, even while the rest of the network is steadily nationalised.

It is about time. Eurostar has, just like any monopoly, become complacent and expensive.

When I checked on Friday afternoon, if I wanted to book a trip to Paris for the following weekend, the cheapest return train ticket would cost me £260 return, and well over £300 if I decided to travel at a civilised hour, while I could get a return easyJet flight for £110.

The prices are way too high, especially given that most of the infrastructure is now three decades old. It is not as if the service is amazing either, even if it recently unveiled spiffy new gender neutral uniforms for the staff.

On Trustpilot, the train service has an average review ranking of just 2.1 out of a possible 5.0, which suggests, to put it politely, that there is a little room for improvement.

That is not nearly good enough. The entire point of the tunnel was to replace flights with train routes, both to reduce congestion at the airports and to cut carbon emissions. The train service should have driven the budget airlines out of the market on that route by now.

Instead, eye-watering ticket prices mean many of us prefer to get a cheap flight instead. As for the plans for seamless train journeys that would link into the Continent’s increasingly sophisticated high-speed rail network, and whisk us all effortlessly around France, and on to Germany, Italy or Spain, they remain on the drawing board, and have done for decades.

It is a huge missed opportunity, and one that will only start to be fixed once the tunnel is finally opened up to competition.

Here is the problem, however. Why only allow Virgin to offer an alternative service? There were three more bidders for the route who were turned down.

As well as Virgin, Gemini Trains, chaired by the Labour peer Lord Berkeley, as well as Trenalitia, the main operating arm of the Italian rail operator and the Spanish start-up Evolyn, were all interested in bidding for the route.

Instead of just one new operator, we could have had four or five. Sure, there are issues with hangar space. But even in Sir Sadiq Khan’s London, where virtually nothing gets built any more, it is surely not impossible to build some extra storage capacity for the trains if that is what is needed, and if some extra platform space is needed at St Pancras, then surely that would be possible as well.

In reality, a whole series of competitors would genuinely kickstart the market. We need a range of different services: a British Airways-style business class offering, for example, for all the people travelling to Brussels or Paris on expenses, alongside a much cheaper Ryanair-style service for people who are getting away for the weekend and would rather spend their money on dinner out than the train fare.

Sure, not all of us want to make a seven or eight-hour rail trip to Milan, or take a 10-hour through-train to Madrid, but a few people will, partly because they want to cut their carbon footprint, or perhaps because they just happen, perfectly understandably, to prefer the old-fashioned romance of the train to the cattle-car conditions of most airports.

We could even see direct routes from other British cities, perhaps with trains running from Manchester straight through to Paris, or else from Birmingham direct to Brussels.

The important point is this: we can’t know for certain which routes would be profitable or what the levels of demand might be.

But we can be sure of one thing: if we allow lots of different operators access to the rail tracks running under the English Channel, then plenty of different possible routes will be tried out, and we will find out very quickly which ones work and which ones don’t.

A duopoly is a lot better than a monopoly, but only just. It can very quickly slip back into a cosy cartel, with both companies quietly agreeing to keep prices high and to keep the competition limited. It is less hassle and more profitable.

In reality, the tunnel has been a squandered asset. It should be opened up to a whole range of different services, and it will be far better off from a blast of fierce competition between competitors.

data.title: Heathrow boss buys ‘extra-loud phone’ after sleeping through airport blackout
data.description:
The boss of Heathrow bought himself an “extra-loud phone” after sleeping through multiple calls when a power outage led to the closure of the airport.

Thomas Woldbye was asleep when his senior staff decided to suspend all flights because of a fire at Hayes electrical substation, which supplies about a third of Heathrow’s electricity needs.

The blaze started at Europe’s busiest airport late on the evening of March 20 and flights were suspended until about 6pm the next day.

A Heathrow-commissioned inquiry led by Ruth Kelly, the former transport secretary, found Mr Woldbye was not woken by alerts sent to his mobile, which was on a bedside table, because it had gone into silent mode “without him being aware”.

Asked at the Airlines 2025 conference in Westminster on Monday if he had since obtained an “extra-loud phone”, Mr Woldbye replied: “Oh, absolutely. And more.”

‘Nothing should depend on me alone’

He said he was “personally devastated” by his phone being on silent, describing it as a “technical glitch”.

But he added that “nothing in this world should be depending on me alone” and “all the right people were there to take the right decisions”.

More than 270,000 passengers’ journeys were disrupted by the incident.

Mr Woldbye, who was paid £3.2m last year, said businesses “need to be able to trust our utilities” and we are “not in a country where everybody needs a back-up”.

He added: “The Neso [National Energy System Operator] report made it very, very clear there were major failures on the part of National Grid and they should have been fixed.

“We should not have been in that situation in the first place.”

The Heathrow boss was also asked whether a similar power outage today would close the airport down again.

He replied: “It would, probably, for about six hours.”

Mr Woldbye added that “lots of procedures” had changed since March to ensure that recovering from another sudden power loss would be “faster”.

One plan included installing “more backup generators”, following criticisms that the airport had replaced diesel-fired equipment with a net zero-compliant biomass plant, which needs mains electricity for it to be started up.

data.title: Accounting giant cuts partners’ pay by £92,000
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest accountancy firms has cut partner pay by £92,000, as it blamed falling profits on a downturn in the UK economy.

BDO announced on Tuesday that annual partner payouts were down 13.5pc in 2024, falling from £681,000 to £589,000.

This stemmed from company-wide profits falling by 7.5pc to £210m, with revenues flat at £1bn as it struggled with a drop in activity across both its audit and deals advisory divisions.

Mark Shaw, BDO managing partner, said: “The well-documented economic headwinds across the UK have resulted in a challenging year for the profession as a whole. Growth rates have slowed and profitability has come under pressure.”

It comes amid growing pressure on the UK’s services sector, which is battling weaker demand.

As the fifth-largest accountancy firm in the UK, behind the likes of Deloitte and EY, BDO is focused largely on serving small and medium-sized businesses.

This has left it exposed to a slowdown in the broader economy, with many companies battling higher tax rises under Labour.

Hiring spree backlash

The slump in BDO’s profits was made worse by a decision to embark on a hiring spree last year, which was in stark contrast to its rivals.

The likes of PwC, KPMG and Deloitte have all cut jobs in recent months, as they scramble to keep costs at bay by replacing more roles with artificial intelligence.

This has led to many of the Big Four also scaling back graduate schemes to automate more low-level work.

By contrast, BDO increased the size of its trainee cohort this year and brought 36 new partners into the fold, while also handing out promotions to 2,440 staff.

Unlike BDO, PwC, EY, Deloitte and KPMG all increased their partner payouts last year.

It comes after BDO was fined £6.5m last month after the regulator found that one of its former staff members had forged signatures on audit reports.

This fuelled scrutiny of the company’s recent audit performance, having been repeatedly admonished by the regulator in recent years.

data.title: Channel Tunnel owner blames tax rises as it scraps UK investment
data.description:
The owner of the Channel Tunnel has scrapped all UK investment following the Government’s decision to burden it with tens of millions of pounds in extra taxes.

Eurotunnel said plans to increase its business rates bill by almost 200pc were “unjustified and confiscatory in nature”.

As a result, it said it was suspending plans to develop a freight terminal in Barking, east London, which would have allowed it to run dedicated freight services from France and free up capacity on passenger routes.

The Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which provides the valuations across England and Wales that underpin property taxes, has informed the company that it plans to almost triple the rateable value of its properties from next year.

This would increase Eurotunnel’s annual business rates bill from £22m to £65m in the coming years, although the increase would be phased in, meaning its bill in 2026 will initially jump to £35m.

The VOA has been tasked with overhauling business rates by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, as she wants companies with bigger premises to pay more in levies to help reduce costs for smaller sites.

“Eurotunnel expresses its deep disagreement with this planned increase and considers it is unjustified and confiscatory in nature, and that it threatens Eurotunnel’s investment capacity in Great Britain,” the company said on Thursday.

“Eurotunnel will continue its efforts to negotiate with the VOA and its supervisory authorities until the final valuation decision, expected at the end of March 2026.”

Yann Leriche, the chief executive, told the Financial Times that the move “makes all of our investments loss-making, so we won’t be making any more investments”.

He added: “As of today, we have frozen our investment in rail assets in the UK.”

He said the suspended investment amounted to £15m, while Eurotunnel suggested it could challenge the decision.

Cancelling plans to boost freight through the Channel Tunnel would conflict with the Government’s hopes of taking cargo off the roads by boosting rail.

“Eurotunnel believes that it is currently unduly penalised compared to its competitors whose activities are more carbon-intensive, less-taxed, and some of which are deviating from the social models applicable in France and Great Britain,” the company said.

Earlier this week, the boss of airline Jet2 complained that the Government was treating the travel industry like a “cash cow”.

This month, electric vehicle charging companies wrote to the Treasury warning that plans to introduce business rates on chargers’ parking bays would hurt the adoption of EVs.

A VOA spokesman said: “The VOA is responsible for non-domestic valuations which reflect changes in the property market. All valuations are carried out by experienced professionals in accordance with industry best practice and legal requirements. The VOA does not determine business rates and next year’s liability has not yet been confirmed.

“We have engaged with Getlink and their advisers on multiple occasions over the past 18 months to discuss their valuation and fully explain our approach. These discussions remain ongoing and we are committed to continuing constructive engagement.

“Ratepayers have the right to formally challenge their valuation with the VOA if they believe it is inaccurate. Should they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may appeal to the independent Valuation Tribunal.”

data.title: Desperate Macron is now ready to use the French media to stifle dissent
data.description:
Emmanuel Macron has long been fixated by fake news and “disinformation”. In a speech in January 2018, he promised to “develop our legal means of protecting democracy against fake news”, singling out the threat from Russia. This obsession, however, is now being used to punish his enemies and marginalise media he dismisses as “far Right”.

This is why, on Monday, Macron’s office denied in a social media post that the president was planning an Orwellian “ministry of truth”. It also rubbished the notion that he had a soft spot for Pravda – the official newspaper of the Soviet Union.

The president was forced on the defensive after a barrage of justified criticism over the weekend from the conservative press and political class. They were alarmed at a speech made by Macron last week in which he declared his wish to introduce a “professional certification” of the media in order to distinguish between what is fact and what is fake.

Macron has stressed that he would not issue the certification of veracity but it would be done by the journalists themselves. This would include greater powers to take broadcasters off air.

In February this year one of France’s most popular free-to-air stations, C8, was shut down by the regulator; the decision had nothing to do with Russian interference but because its editorial control allegedly didn’t respect individual rights.

Many on the Right, including Marine Le Pen, suspected the real reason was because C8 was more populist than progressive. It was, she claimed, a “worrying decision that rules in favour of the Ayatollahs of group-think”.

C8 was part of the media group owned by Vincent Bolloré, the conservative tycoon who is the bête noire of the French elite. His other media interests include a Sunday newspaper, a radio station called Europe 1 and CNews, a television station that has in a short space of time become the most popular source of news in France.

Its success enrages progressives. They refuse to appear on CNews because they claim it is “far Right”. By “far Right”, they mean it doesn’t shy away from confronting troubling questions about mass immigration and insecurity. On the other hand France Televisions, the state broadcaster, prefers not to tackle such sensitive issues.

France’s state-owned radio stations are also tainted by Left-wing bias. A conservative think-tank used artificial intelligence throughout October to monitor the ideological orientation during the morning output of two stations, France Inter and France Culture. France Inter was 60 per cent Left-wing and 16 per cent Right-wing, and France Culture was 66 to six in favour of the Left.

This came as little surprise. In 2023 the head of France Inter, Adèle Van Reeth, said she was proud that the station was “progressive”.

Did she consult the French taxpayer about this ideological orientation? Evidently not, which is perhaps one reason why France Inter has lost 460,000 listeners this year. Public television is also shedding viewers as the French flock instead to independent broadcasters, particularly those which lean to the Right. They don’t believe they spread false information; they believe they tell inconvenient truths that the elite would rather not hear.

CNews doesn’t champion Marine Le Pen but nor does it knock her. It gives her a platform, as does her protégé, Jordan Bardella, increasingly tipped to replace Le Pen as the National Rally’s presidential candidate in 2027.

Bardella said that Macron’s idea to tighten media control was “tampering with freedom of expression”. It was, added Bardella, the act of a president who “has lost power and seeks to maintain it by controlling information”. That’s a truth unlikely to be heard in Macron’s new ministry.

data.title: Hotel tycoon attacks Heathrow boss over runway plans
data.description:
Hotel tycoon Surinder Arora has accused Heathrow bosses of “propagating myths” about his bid to build a third runway at Europe’s busiest airport.

Mr Arora said Heathrow had waged a campaign to undermine his proposals for a shorter, cheaper strip than the one proposed by the airport itself. He called on Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, to debate the rival plans.

The tycoon said Heathrow falsely claimed his scheme would mean the demolition of “a crazy number of houses” and require a motorway spur from the M4 to the airport to be lowered beneath the proposed runway, interfering with railway lines.

Mr Arora shot back and claimed the airport had failed to explain how its own longer runway would span the M25 without the rebuilding of many more road links that it had admitted to.

The tussle over the rival runway bids has turned personal days before ministers are due to decide which of the two to take forward as part of Labour’s plan to encourage long-term growth through big-ticket infrastructure projects.

Heathrow has proposed a 3,500-metre strip to the north west of the current runways. While it would lift capacity to 150 million passengers a year, it would cost £49bn to deliver and involve redirecting the M25 under the new runway.

Meanwhile, Mr Arora, who owns hotels around Heathrow, has proposed building a 2,800-metre runway with a price tag of no more than £25bn that would avoid the M25 and, he claims, accommodate almost as many flights as the airport’s own blueprint.

He said: “Our dear friends at Heathrow will always try to propagate myths and doubts about us. But when it comes to their own scheme, it’s smoke and mirrors.

“If Thomas Woldbye wants to stand up in public and have a debate, I’m more than happy to do that, because we’ve got nothing to hide.

“People said to me at the start, are you sure you want to do this? It’s David against Goliath. Heathrow are a big machine, they’ve got big shareholders and they’re going to try to blow you away. And that’s what they’ve been trying to do.”

The Department for Transport (DfT) is expected to announce its preferred option for the third runway by the end of the month. It is understood that it may do so before the Budget in order to give Chancellor Rachel Reeves the opportunity to champion the project.

As a decision looms, Heathrow revealed the chief executives of 13 suppliers and terminal retailers had written to Heidi Alexander, the transport secretary, expressing “full support” for its plans. The companies included retailers Burberry and Fortnum & Mason and builder Costain.

Mr Arora said Heathrow was “clutching onto straws” after seeing support for its runway plan, part of a £49.1bn expansion scheme, ebbing away.

Sean Doyle, the chief executive of British Airways, said last week his company, the biggest airline at Heathrow, was open to a shorter runway and said if the M25 could be avoided, it should be.

Mr Arora said: “They never thought they would be in this position, having to justify their existence. But the train has left the station. All their behaviour over the last decades and their abuse of their monopoly and lack of value for money for customers and airlines, all that is coming out.

“People are beginning to realise that the regulatory system is broken and that the track record of these guys is so bad. Everybody is fed up and saying enough is enough.

“If I was knocking on your door with that track record and saying I am the most expensive builder and you’re going to get crap service, because I’m not even in the top 20 in the world, wouldn’t you slam the door in my face?”

Carlton Brown, Arora Group’s finance chief, said he expected the decision on the runway to turn on whether or not the Government trusts Heathrow to build a strip over the M25.

He said: “There is only one real question: can you avoid the M25 or not? That is the problem the DfT now faces, because we have shown definitively you can do it.”

A Heathrow spokesman said its plans been closely scrutinised for a decade and previously won the support of Parliament, “so it’s hardly smoke and mirrors.”

He said the notion that Heathrow was delivering a poor service passenger “is not reflective of reality,” with the hub the most punctual in Europe and ranked 22nd out of more than 500 worldwide according to Skytrax passenger surveys.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Cheap wine slump causes £340m hangover for Blossom Hill owner
data.description:
A slump in demand for cheap wine has dealt a $450m (£340m) blow to the maker of Blossom Hill.

Melbourne-based Treasury Wine Estates (TWE), whose brands also include Penfolds and Lindemans, has written down the value of its US business due to the poor performance of its low-cost brands, despite higher-end wines continuing to sell well.

The write-down comes amid slowing demand for so-called “corner shop wines”. Sales of wines priced between £4 and £6.49 have fallen by 5pc since 2019 in the UK, according to industry analysts IWSR, even as the sales volume of bottles priced from £17.78 to £33.75 grew by 2pc.

Data firm NIQ similarly found that Hardys Wine, once the highest-grossing wine brand in Britain, lost almost £50m in sales over the year to August.

Bryan Roberts, of retail analyst IGD, said shoppers “seeking to recreate restaurant or bar experiences more affordably” were increasingly willing to spend more on premium wines to drink at home, rather than budget alternatives.

Andrew Ingham, of wholesaler Bibendum Wine, said hospitality venues were similarly opting for higher-end wines, which are easier to preserve, as customers increasingly order by the glass.

A shift in drinking habits

He added: “In situations where a group might once have shared a bottle of wine, it’s increasingly common for one person in that group to abstain entirely, whether for cost, health or lifestyle reasons.”

Just 14pc of UK adults drink four or more times a week, compared with 18pc in 2018, according to a survey by Drinkaware.

Mr Ingham added that households were going out less often, but that “when they do, they want the experience to feel more special than it used to.”

He said: “Someone who used to go out for dinner twice a month might now go out less frequently, but when they do, they expect a better overall experience, including a higher-quality selection of food and wine.”

Mr Ingham said the “premiumisation” of wine “had been a trend for some time”. He added: “Shrinkflation isn’t really possible in wine, and costs continue to rise. For years, it was easy to find bottles at £4, but that era is largely over.”

Only one in five shoppers prioritises a low price when buying wine, according to market analyst Mintel.

Sam Fischer, formerly of drinks company Lion Group, was appointed as TWE’s chief executive in October. Mr Fischer is expected to visit the company’s US operations next week.

The US downturn is disappointing for TWE. The company bet on the market to fuel growth after China imposed punitive tariffs on Australian fine wines in 2020.

data.title: The BBC needs to acknowledge that it has a problem, and it’s not Gibb and Prescott
data.description:
In evidence to the Commons culture committee, the chairman of the BBC, Samir Shah, apologised to viewers and staff for the mistakes the Corporation had made and the impact this had on public trust. In particular he referred to the editing of a Donald Trump speech on Panorama before the US election last year. But there was a sense that his concern and that of Labour MPs was about process not culture.

Mr Shah said these issues should have been addressed earlier when the Board first knew about them. But the reason for this delay needs to be explained. Michael Prescott, the author of the memorandum published by The Telegraph earlier this month, said it was all of a piece with the BBC’s reluctance to act on complaints about impartiality made by its own editorial standards committee. 

Mr Prescott made clear his love of the BBC and admiration for Tim Davie, the director-general who has since announced his resignation along with the head of news Deborah Turness. 

Furthermore, he did not consider the Corporation to be “institutionally biased” and was identifying specific instances of bad editorial practice rather than making a general point. He said the BBC management was too defensive and Mr Davie had a “blind spot” over editorial failings. 

Mr Shah and the Board did nothing until The Telegraph exposed what had gone on. Labour MPs seemed determined to show that Mr Prescott – and Board member Sir Robbie Gibb – were politically biased but failed to lay a glove on them. Mr Prescott’s main concern was that the BBC did not implement the recommendations of its own editorial reviews and managers appeared to have a systemic antipathy to doing so.

Mr Prescott was arguably being charitable in attributing this to a failure of process. Others may see it as a matter of bias against Right-wing opinion since it was largely this that was affected by a perceived lack of impartiality. Indeed, in the run-up to the Commons hearings, the BBC hardly mentioned in its own coverage that the whole controversy was to do with allegations of bias as opposed to governance or political plotting.

There was praise at the committee for the internal BBC discussions around some of these concerns, as though this was a singular virtue when measured against other media outlets. But it is an absolute requirement because, unlike its rivals, the BBC is publicly funded. That is why it is so closely scrutinised – and rightly so.

data.title: Virgin Media fined £24m after switching off elderly customers’ landlines
data.description:
Virgin Media has been fined £23.8m after rushing to switch off elderly customers’ landlines.

Ofcom said the company had put thousands of vulnerable customers at “direct risk of harm” by moving them to digital landlines.

The company received the penalty after it admitted it had disconnected customers with so-called “telecare” devices – personal alarms designed to be activated in a health emergency or after a fall. These devices are connected to a person’s landline, which rings emergency services if the button if pressed.

Virgin Media flagged “a number of serious incidents” in November and December 2023 as it switched customers from the UK’s old copper landline network to the digital alternative.

The regulator found that the company failed to properly identify and record the status of telecare customers, “resulting in significant gaps in the screening process”.

Ofcom said that thousands of vulnerable customers had their telecare alarms effectively switched off between April 2022 and December 2023 while the disconnection was in place.

Ian Strawhorne, the regulator’s director of enforcement, said: “It’s unacceptable that vulnerable customers were put at direct risk of harm and left without appropriate support by Virgin Media, during what should have been a safe and straightforward upgrade to their landline services.

“Today’s fine makes clear to companies that, if they fail to protect their vulnerable customers, they can expect to face similar enforcement action.”

Telecom companies are in the process of switching customers from traditional copper-based networks to digital alternatives.

The ageing copper networks have become increasingly unstable, leading to an increase in outages. Many parts required for copper networks are also no longer produced, making it harder to fix faults.

However, the switchover has proved controversial given many older and vulnerable people rely on their landlines. It has led to concerns about possible harm as the networks are changed.

Dennis Reed, director of Silver Voices, a senior citizens campaign group, said it “has continuing concerns that the switch to digital landlines is compromising the safety of vulnerable customers”.

He said: “Many such customers may be confused, frail or ill and if they fail to contact their telecom supplier they will find their traditional landline cut off and receive a router through the post that they have to set up themselves.

“They will find in an emergency that their phone is dead. In a dereliction of government responsibility, this major infrastructure project has been left to the commercial operators to implement, under weak national guidelines.

“Ministers have refused my many requests to discuss the challenges of this botched and rushed programme. Unfortunately further lives will be lost while the Government and telecom companies cross their fingers that there will be no further tragedies.”

Virgin Media has previously complained that telecare companies and local governments were not doing enough to prepare for the digital switch, leaving elderly and vulnerable at risk. Rob Orr, Virgin Media O2’s chief operating officer, said in 2023 that councils and telecare companies needed to “step up”.

Virgin Media has four weeks to pay the fine, which will then be passed on to the Treasury.

A spokesman for the company said it had updated its procedures since 2023 and introduced further safeguards to improve its treatment of vulnerable customers.

It said: “While historically the majority of migrations were completed without issue, we recognise that we didn’t get everything right and have since addressed the migration issues identified by Ofcom.”

This includes a package of support for vulnerable customers including improved communications, additional at-home support and extensive checks following the switchover, as well as taking part in a national awareness campaign.

The company also stressed that it was “essential we move our customers to digital services” from traditional analogue landlines which are “less reliable and difficult to maintain”.

data.title: Savings tax calculator: How much more will you pay?
data.description:
The tax rate on income earned from savings will increase by 2p from April 2027.

Savings income above the annual allowance of £1,000 for basic-rate payers will be taxed at 22pc, while higher and additional-rate earners will pay 42pc and 47pc respectively above the tax-free limit of £500.

This tool can help you calculate how much tax you’ll need to pay on your savings interest.

Tax on savings interest calculator

How to use this calculator

Use the labelled boxes to enter the amount of savings you have, and the interest rate being paid.

Then, tick the relevant box to indicate whether you’re a basic, higher or additional-rate taxpayer. This is the highest rate of tax you pay, and will depend on your total income.

If you have savings split into more than one account, you may want to repeat this exercise to calculate the potential tax due for each account.

How it works

The calculator works out the amount of savings interest you’ll earn over the course of a year, based on the assumption that your savings amount and level of interest stays the same for this duration.

In its results, you’ll see the “pre-tax return” – that is, the total amount of savings interest you’ll earn for the year.

This is set against your personal savings allowance, which is determined by on the income tax band you’ve chosen. This is how the calculator works out how much tax you’ll pay, and your total savings interest return after tax.

What is this calculator useful for?

This calculator could be useful for anyone who needs a quick and easy way to gauge whether they are likely to pay tax on their savings interest.

It is particularly useful following Rachel Reeves’s decision to increase tax rates on savings by two percentage points from April 2027. It means basic rate taxpayers will pay 22pc, higher rate will pay 42pc and additional ratepayers face a 47pc rate.

More savers are likely to be caught in this tax net following the Chancellor’s move to limit the amount most people can save into a cash Isa to £12,000 a year.

However, there are other ways to avoid paying tax on your savings.

Tax on savings interest is becoming a reality for growing numbers of savers, thanks to accounts offering higher rates over the past couple of years, and the fact that increasing number of people are being dragged into higher tax bands due to years of frozen thresholds.

How to pay tax on savings

You’ll only need to pay tax on savings interest if you’ve exceeded both your personal allowance and your personal savings allowance. If you’re on a lower income of less than £17,570 you may also qualify for the savings starting rate, too.

If you need to pay tax on your savings, you can:

  • Notify HMRC – you can do this voluntarily even if you are not required to do so. Telling HMRC about your savings interest could speed up the process of working out how much tax you owe.
  • Wait for a tax code change – as banks and building societies are required to report savings interest to HMRC, if you are employed or receive income from a private pension then the tax authority can work out the tax you owe and then recover it by changing your PAYE tax code.
  • Report and pay by self-assessment – if you already file a tax return, you’ll be required to report your savings interest as part of the self-assessment process. Your final tax bill will take any savings interest into account. If your savings interest is more than £10,000, you must file a tax return regardless of whether or not you usually do this.

data.title: Homeowner sues over claim pylons knocked £125,000 off property value
data.description:
A homeowner is taking legal action against energy giant SSE, claiming that plans for new electricity pylons near his home knocked £125,000 off the value of the property.

Matt Gready claims he was on the verge of selling his family home in the Aberdeenshire countryside for £700,000 when energy giant SSE announced plans for a line of pylons within 230 metres of the property.

The plans prompted a buyer to withdraw and subsequently knocked £125,000 off the value of the property when it did eventually sell, he said.

Mr Gready is now seeking compensation for himself and thousands of other residents of the Aberdeenshire countryside over the impact of SSE’s pylon plans on their property values.

He pointed out that SSE, one of three UK transmission line operators, has pledged to “champion a fair and just energy transition” to clean power.

“SSE is building the value of its own asset portfolio while destroying the value of thousands of family homes – how can that be part of a ‘fair and just’ transition?” he said.

Mr Gready owned a property near the village of Dunecht, which is along the route of a 350-pylon scheme linking Kintore, Aberdeenshire, and Tealing, Angus. The project has attracted 10,600 objections.

Details of the plans were published while Mr Gready was in the process of selling his house.

He said: “The sale collapsed because the buyer withdrew, citing the pylon development – a fact confirmed in solicitor correspondence. After seven more buyers pulled out, I was forced to sell at £575,000.

“I asked SSE to compensate me for my loss, but despite submitting a pre-action letter and a without prejudice settlement offer, SSE has refused to engage, denied all liability, rejected dispute resolution, and now redirected all future correspondence to its legal team.

“They told me they didn’t want to offer compensation for fear of setting a precedent, but their plans caused me a direct loss of £125,000 and SSE should compensate me for that.”

Alexander Burnett, the Aberdeenshire West MSP, has written to both SSE and the Scottish Government, calling for a dedicated compensation fund for those impacted by the Kintore to Tealing power link.

Mr Burnett said: “Residents living in the shadow of this proposed pylon route are having their lives turned upside down by these hugely damaging plans. Mr Gready was forced to sell his property for £125,000 less than if these proposals didn’t exist.

“Neither SSE nor the Government have any understanding of the magnitude of this issue and the misery it is causing for communities who are fearing for their future.”

The Kintore to Tealing pylon link is part of SSE’s £22bn Pathway to 2030 investment programme. The new pylons will carry electricity from wind farms in remote areas of Scotland to the power-hungry cities in southern Scotland and England. The Scottish and UK governments have both said such schemes are essential to decarbonising the UK electricity system.

Power transmission companies argue that their infrastructure has little or no impact on property values. They argue that local people soon get used to the visual intrusion and low-level buzzing of electrical installations.

However, this view is contradicted by research from the London School of Economics (LSE), published last year.

Prof Steve Gibbons and Cheng Keat Tang, a former PhD student, found that the values of homes within 250 metres of new transmission lines fell by more than 20pc – implying a drop of £126,000 for a £600,000 home. This decline is very close to what Mr Gready says he experienced.

The researchers said: “The costs of compensating households for the construction of land-based pylons could become very large. Compensating just 10pc of the 28 million households in the UK for pylons within 1500 metres would cost £24bn in 2024 prices.

The researchers warned of a similar destructive impact from wind and solar farms.

Mr Gready said: “As the UK accelerates its energy transition, it’s important that individuals directly affected by major infrastructure projects are not left without redress.

“Strengthening ethical governance will be essential if the just transition is to be more than just words.”

An SSE spokesman said: “We’re sorry to hear of Mr Gready’s concerns and have been in regular dialogue with him to better understand these. In this case, we don’t consider that a legal basis has been established to substantiate an award of compensation.

“In developing our proposals, we have carefully balanced a range of environmental, technical and economic considerations.”

data.title: JP Morgan to spend £3bn on new Canary Wharf tower
data.description:
JP Morgan has announced plans to build a £3bn new headquarters in Canary Wharf in a major boost to London’s financial district and the Government.

The US banking giant has revealed plans for a three million square foot office that will hold 12,000 people and serve as its main European base.

The office is set to be one of Europe’s biggest towers. JP Morgan’s new London office, designed by Foster + Partners, will be more than double the floorspace of Britain’s tallest building, The Shard, and significantly larger than the next-biggest tower block by floor area in London, 22 Bishopsgate.

The announcement comes a day after Rachel Reeves’s Budget, which laid out a series of tax increases that will hit middle earners. However, a rumoured raid on bank profits failed to materialise, sending bank stocks surging yesterday.

It is understood JP Morgan’s decision to press ahead with the tower was unaffected by the Budget, with a final decision taken weeks before today’s announcement.

Jamie Dimon, JP Morgan’s chief executive, said the Canary Wharf tower represented the bank’s “lasting commitment to the city, the UK, our clients and our people”.

He added: “The UK Government’s priority of economic growth has been a critical factor in helping us make this decision.”

Ms Reeves, the Chancellor, said JP Morgan’s expansion was a “multi-billion pound vote of confidence in the UK economy”.

The announcement is a boost for the Chancellor after the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) concluded that her tax-and-spend Budget would do nothing to improve growth. It has sparked claims that Ms Reeves has abandoned her mission to boost the economy.

The new JP Morgan building is estimated to cost £3bn and the US bank said it would contribute £10bn to the local economy over six years. It said the project, situated on the Riverside development complex, would create 7,800 local jobs in construction and other industries.

Mr Dimon, regarded as the most powerful banker on Wall Street, has consistently shown support for the Labour Government and praised the approach taken by both Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, and Ms Reeves.

JP Morgan has operated out of Canary Wharf’s 25 Bank Street, the former UK headquarters of Lehman Brothers, since 2012. The bank said it would also launch a small-scale refurbishment of this office to aid staff while the new tower is being built.

The bank has owned the riverside plot where its new tower will be built for over a decade and had considered building a new tower there in 2010.

Building a new office was seen as a better option than either a major renovation of the current building or moving into new premises in the City of London. 

It is understood that renovating JP Morgan’s current Canary Wharf could have cost more than £1bn.

The planned expansion will be a boon to Canary Wharf as London’s financial hub grapples with high vacancy rates amid the rise of working from home. Office vacancy rates hit an all-time high earlier this year.

In response, Canary Wharf has been building more housing and attractions in the area in a bid to diversify. However, the district’s high-rise apartments have seen wealthier residents quitting Britain. 

Canary Wharf was also a centre of anti-migrant protests over the summer after asylum seekers were housed at the Britannia Hotel near Canada Square.

JP Morgan’s new tower block project will include expanded public parkland and the redevelopment of the Canary Wharf dock. The office will include modern trading floors as well as perks such as rooftop terraces, wellness areas, restaurants and cafes.

The investment comes as JP Morgan expands in Britain. The bank has been growing its investment banking operations in London and launched its Chase banking app in Britain in 2021, which now has more than 2.6 million customers.

Once the building is completed – which is expected to take six years – JP Morgan’s other London-based staff will relocate to the new block. The bank, which currently has 23,000 employees in the UK, separately announced a £350m expansion of its Bournemouth campus earlier this year.

Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, said: “This major commitment will create jobs and further strengthen London’s position at the centre of the global economy.”

data.title: Labour plots blocks of flats near green belt train stations
data.description:
Labour is plotting a green belt building blitz with plans to make it far easier to build blocks of flats near commuter stations.

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, is preparing to grant default approval for building projects near well-connected train stations.

The new rules will extend to land within the green belt and include minimum housing density standards, implying properties must contain multiple dwellings.

The housing department said this was intended to “make the most of sustainable growth opportunities for local housing, jobs and businesses”.

Councils will be forced to inform the Government if they intend to block a project delivering 150 homes or more, giving ministers the opportunity to step in and overrule the decision.

As part of the plans, ministers are also preparing to scrap rules that require Sport England, The Gardens Trust and Theatres Trust to be consulted on construction projects.

Mr Reed said: “We’re making it easier to build well-connected and high-quality homes, using stronger powers to speed things up if councils drag their feet, and proposing to streamline the consultation process to cut back delays.”

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said: “We’re ending years of dither and delay by green lighting affordable new homes for working people.”

‘Deeply concerning’

However, the proposals sparked an immediate backlash from countryside and heritage groups.

John Watkins, chair of The Gardens Trust, said: “Precious parks and gardens, which have both grown and developed over many centuries, would be lost to communities both now and in future.

“It is also incredibly frustrating and disappointing that after intimating that the launch of this consultation was imminent back in February, the Government has delayed, delayed and delayed again – creating a huge amount of uncertainty and distraction.”

Mr Watkins said the trust would “robustly contest the proposal”.

Becky Pullinger, head of land use planning at The Wildlife Trusts, called the plans “deeply concerning”.

“Whilst we must seek opportunities to build homes that integrate with sustainable transport and existing infrastructure, local authorities must be enabled to do this – not ordered to do so, regardless of local circumstance,” she said.

“This erosion of local democracy will not only strip away communities’ ability to have a say in the types of homes and infrastructure they need, but also threatens to override the safeguards in place which protect the wildlife, nature and greenspaces local residents know and love.”

Alice Roberts of CPRE, the countryside charity, said the decision was “disastrous” for traffic congestion, adding that it contradicted the Government’s net zero ambitions.

She said: “Evidence shows the vast majority of trips in such developments will be made by car as the train only goes in a specific direction and stops only at specific locations.

“It is hypocritical for the Government to say it wants ‘sustainable’ development and then green light car-dependent development in this way.”

David Simmonds MP, shadow housing minister, said: “It’s clear Britain needs more homes. But instead of trying to work with local communities to deliver homes people actually want, Labour are trying to railroad through unpopular developments.”

The new proposals come as Mr Reed strives to meet Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of parliament. Housebuilders have said they do not believe the Government will meet the target.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour forced to use pensioners to build thousands of homes
data.description:
Labour is relying on an army of “Bob the Builder” pensioners to deliver its target of 1.5 million new homes by 2029, a new report has warned.

The study by the Centre for Social Justice (CSJ) warned “Bob the Builder is heading for retirement” as it found the number of construction workers aged 65 or over has nearly tripled from 49,361 to 130,791 since 2008 and now accounts for one in 20 of all employees in the industry.

It warned the ageing profile of construction workers, with more than one in three now over 50, threatened to exacerbate a recruitment crisis that could derail Labour’s housebuilding plans. By 2050, the average age of a construction worker will be over 46 years old, according to the CSJ.

The report estimated that the target of 1.5 million new homes by 2029 will require an additional 161,000 workers, rising to over 239,000 once factoring in wider projected demand by 2030.

But the think tank warned this housebuilding target was extremely unlikely to be achieved as the construction sector’s “workforce has concurrently aged and diminished in size”.

There are now some 250,000 fewer builders than there were before the Covid-19 pandemic and Brexit. With many likely to quit the industry because of age, construction is relying on a diminishing number of younger workers without sufficient skills training programmes to replenish the industry.

Compared with 2008, there are two-fifths fewer 16 to 24-year-olds working in construction, a fall of more than 150,000. This trend has accelerated since before the Covid-19 pandemic, with over 30,000 fewer 16 to 24-year-olds in the industry compared with six years ago.

The CSJ blamed previous governments for attempting to “plug gaps” through immigration, saying that “the reliance on migration has contributed to a failure to tackle the root causes of the domestic construction workforce’s long-term decline”.

It said the root causes were “a dysfunctional industry labour model” – where workers were laid off and then left the industry for good every time there was a recession – and “a broken education and training system which fails to equip learners with the skills they need to enter employment”.

The CSJ recommended that the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) should introduce a workforce credit – a £670m effective tax cut worth 30 per cent of a young construction worker’s salary, paid for by reforming health benefits.

It said the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) should go further and faster on planning reform by rolling out design programmes to get Britain building.

The Department for Education (DfE) should bring construction into secondary schools by introducing a new technical and vocational option at GCSE level, it said. The DfE, DWP and MHCLG should launch a nationwide ‘earn as you train’ campaign to upskill economically inactive people, and low-skilled construction workers.

The CSJ said the Home Office should resist calls by some firms in the construction industry to relax immigration rules and re-introduce the resident labour market test, where companies are required to advertise a job first in the UK before seeking to hire overseas.

Josh Nicholson, senior researcher at the CSJ, said: “Fixing Britain’s construction-skills shortage has never been more urgent. The construction workforce is in near-terminal decline, but today we are publishing a plan to turn this crisis around.

“Unless the Government and industry can work together to reverse these trends, then it is highly unlikely Britain will be able to build the housing and infrastructure it so desperately needs.”

Jonathan Hinder, the Labour MP, said: “Britain’s prosperity depends on its construction workforce, yet we lack workers by the thousands. For too long Britain has undervalued the contribution of construction workers to our society and economy.

“The Government wants to ‘build, baby, build’. To do so we need workers, workers, workers. Thousands of working-class young people, especially boys, could have their life chances transformed through a rewarding career in construction if we get this right.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ozempic maker wins High Court ban on fake fat jab websites
data.description:
The drugmaker behind Wegovy has secured a major victory after the High Court granted it a blocking order for websites selling fake versions of its weight-loss jabs.

Novo Nordisk will be able to force internet providers to block access to four websites that the company accused of selling “fake and unauthorised forms of semaglutide”, the ingredient which helps people lose weight.

It claimed the websites were selling counterfeit versions of Ozempic, the drugmaker’s diabetes injection, which is used off-label for weight loss.

Novo Nordisk warned the counterfeit drugs “pose a risk to health” and were not regulated, adding: “The substances [the websites] offer to supply have not been evaluated by the UK MHRA [Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency] as being safe or of suitable quality.”

GPs have warned that people are “dicing with death” by using fake injections, with reports of drugs being wrapped up in kitchen roll without instructions when they are sent to customers.

Earlier this month, an investigation found copycat versions were being sold on Facebook and TikTok, with patients who took them reportedly losing vision and vomiting repeatedly. Both social media companies said the sale of weight-loss injections was prohibited under their policies, with TikTok removing searches and hashtags for the drugs after the investigation.

However, there are growing fears that more people could start hunting out dangerous fakes after a major jump in the price of one of the biggest approved obesity jabs.

In September, US drugmaker Eli Lilly increased the UK price of Mounjaro – known as the King Kong of weight-loss injections – by as much as 170pc following pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, to lower the cost for Americans.

It means that the drug now costs as much as £330 per injection pen, based on the highest dose. Each injection pen includes four doses. Previously, the highest-dose injection pen cost £122.

Drug crime specialists at the time warned that the increase was likely to lead to a jump in the number of copycat drugs being imported from Chinese labs and sold illicitly in the UK.

Novo Nordisk has not increased its prices. At an online pharmacy, patients can expect to pay between £99 and £199 for four doses.

It shipped in more supply of its weight-loss jabs to Britain following the price increase at its rival, after seeing more demand.

A spokesman for Novo Nordisk said of the blocking order: “We are pleased that the English High Court has confirmed the availability of website blocking as a remedy to tackle the problem of counterfeit and unauthorised semaglutide.

“This remedy is an innovative and powerful tool for taking effective action and forms an important part of the suite of enforcement remedies available against makers and sellers of unlawful products.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Businessman admits scamming airlines with fake parts
data.description:
An engineering boss has admitted supplying fake parts to airlines such as Ryanair in a scandal that grounded hundreds of planes around the world.

Jose Alejandro Zamora Yrala, the chief executive of UK-based AOG Technics, on Monday pleaded guilty to selling counterfeit aircraft engine parts in a scam that the Serious Fraud Office (SFO) said risked public safety on a global scale.

Mr Yrala, 37, of Virginia Water in Surrey, was granted conditional bail at Southwark Crown Court to appear for his sentencing hearing at the same court on Feb 23.

Between 2019 and 2023, the SFO said Mr Yrala defrauded customers by selling them bogus components that were eventually fitted into the world’s most widely used passenger aircraft engine powering the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

AOG acted as a broker for engine parts, acquiring older models and then falsifying official safety documents to pass them off as new or reconditioned.

Thousands of flights were delayed after the scandal emerged in August 2023 as airlines were forced to ground jets and examine their engines.

The deception began to unravel when a technician at a Portuguese repair centre noticed a discrepancy with a “new” part supplied by AOG, which appeared to be old.

After warnings to British, American and European regulators, Ryanair, Tui, Virgin Australia and nearly every major US carrier had to ground planes to weed out the bogus components.

Emma Luxton, the SFO’s director of operations, said: “This significant and audacious fraud threatened trust in the aviation industry and risked public safety on a global scale.

“I’m extremely proud that we’ve acted swiftly to secure this conviction and wish to thank law enforcement partners in Portugal for their speedy and critical assistance.”

The supply of parts in aviation is done through a patchwork of thousands of small manufacturers, brokers and distributors.

Manufacturers and operators are heavily regulated and largely responsible for orchestrating the paper trail that follows every aviation part, from computers and fuselage panels to screw housings and washers.

Mr Yrala founded AOG in 2015 from a rented terraced house in Hove, West Sussex, before moving the business to London.

The SFO launched its investigation alongside Portuguese authorities in 2023 and the Portuguese case remains ongoing.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ozempic maker suffers boardroom exodus after row with shareholder
data.description:
The wealthy foundation behind Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk has seized control of the company in a boardroom coup after a row over the struggling group’s turnaround.

On Monday, the pharmaceutical giant said chairman Helge Lund and six independent directors would resign and be replaced by a group hand-picked by its biggest shareholder, the Novo Nordisk Foundation.

The drugmaker, which makes the anti-obesity jab Wegovy and Ozempic, said the decision came after a disagreement between the board and the foundation over a plan to rejuvenate the Danish company’s flagging fortunes.

The Novo Nordisk Foundation is the wealthiest charity in the world with £123bn in assets and the company’s largest shareholder, controlling 77pc of Novo.

The foundation has nominated five new directors to be appointed at an emergency meeting next month and a sixth to be appointed next year.

Lars Rebien Sørensen, the former chief executive of Novo, has been nominated to replace Mr Lund as chairman. Mr Lund recently left his position chairing BP after a disastrous attempt to focus the company on green energy.

Novo’s exodus amounts to the entire board, besides the foundation’s own investment boss and the four members elected by employees.

The rift between the company’s top shareholders and its board came to the fore after a slump in Novo’s performance.

The company makes the diabetes drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, which are used for weight loss. Both contain the ingredient semaglutide, which stimulates weight loss by making people feel full.

The initial success of the drugs led Novo to become Europe’s most valuable company, but shares have fallen by two thirds as it has faced competition from Eli Lilly, which makes rival drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Novo’s own successor to Wegovy disappointed in weight loss trials, while the Trump administration has put pressure on the company to cut prices.

“Following dialogue with the Novo Nordisk Foundation regarding the future composition of the board of directors, it has not been possible to reach a common understanding,” Novo said.

“The board proposed a renewal focusing on [the] addition of select, new competencies while also maintaining continuity, whereas the board of the foundation wanted a more extensive reconfiguration.”

It said the new board’s first priority would be “implementing its transformation plans and regaining the company’s growth momentum”.

Mr Sørensen said: “Given the fast-moving environment in which Novo Nordisk operates, we believe that it is in the best interest of the company and its shareholders to implement a board renewal as soon as possible, rather than wait until the ordinary annual general meeting in March next year.”

He said the foundation fully supported the company’s new chief executive, Mike Doustdar, who replaced ousted boss Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen earlier this year, as well as recent plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

Novo’s shares fell by 2.4pc after the announcement.

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: China’s property crisis reignited as state-backed builder fights to avoid default
data.description:
China’s property crisis has reignited after one of its largest state-backed developers was plunged into a fresh debt spiral.

Vanke, once the country’s largest housebuilder, slumped in the bond market on Wednesday amid fears it cannot pay back lenders without government support.

Some of its Chinese-listed bonds plunged by more than 20pc, forcing the Shenzhen Stock Exchange to suspend trading, while its shares also fell 6pc to 3.88 Hong Kong dollars (37p) – the lowest level this year.

Vanke is facing a race against time to convince investors it can repay 13.4bn yuan (£1.4bn) of debts due for repayment next June.

Viewed as a bellwether for China’s property sector, the company’s woes underscore the pressure on the wider market as Beijing attempts to prop up its housing sector.

Demand has remained weak, with new home sales dropping at their fastest monthly pace during October.

This is despite efforts from the Chinese government to stimulate growth, introducing policies to ease curbs on buying in cities and bring down borrowing costs on existing mortgages.

It comes after another property giant, Evergrande Group, delisted from Hong Kong’s stock exchange in August.

The downfall of Evergrande marked the biggest collapse of the sector-wide slump, which had dragged down the country’s economic growth.

Analysts at major banks predict there is more gloom ahead for China’s property market. UBS said it expected property sales, construction activity and investment to drop further for at least two more years.

In October, Fitch Ratings estimated that new home sales could potentially fall by another 15pc to 20pc from their current level before the market stabilises in 2026.

Vanke this year disclosed the sudden departures of Yu Liang, its chairman, and Zhu Jiusheng, its chief executive, citing “work adjustment reasons” for Mr Yu and “health reasons” for Mr Zhu.

At the same time, Vanke said it was experiencing “temporary liquidity difficulties” from a fall in new-build home sales in China. It said it expected to make a loss of 45bn yuan for 2024, dropping from 12bn yuan profit in 2023.

Policymakers in Beijing have reportedly been considering fresh measures to turn around the market’s fortunes, including subsidising interest payments on new mortgages and lowering transaction fees on home sales.

Vanke was contacted for comment.
