data.title: Andy Burnham’s taxpayer-backed Skyscraper tycoon moves to Monaco
data.description:
A wealthy property tycoon who was handed more than £700m in taxpayer loans by Andy Burnham’s Manchester authority has moved to Monaco.

Daren Whitaker, the founder of Manchester development company Renaker, has listed the tax haven as his main residence, according to filings on Companies House.

It comes just months after Mr Whitaker was embroiled in a legal battle over whether he was unfairly lent vast sums by the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA), chaired by the Mayor of Greater Manchester.

This prompted unprecedented scrutiny of his Renaker property empire, which was bankrolled by the GMCA’s Housing Investment Loans Fund to build luxury skyscrapers across Manchester.

In a lawsuit filed by rival developer Aubrey Weis, it was claimed Mr Whitaker was able to cash in on Manchester’s building boom by accessing around £745m in public funds.

This led to allegations that Renaker had effectively benefited from subsidies to build towering blocks that largely contained no affordable housing, some of which were sold to landlords in China.

The lawsuit was ultimately rejected by the UK courts, although not before it had thrust Mr Whitaker and his ties to the GMCA into the spotlight. 

Despite establishing himself as the city’s most prolific developer over the past decade, little is known about Mr Whitaker.

Born in Yorkshire, he started his career at construction company Laing O’Rourke before founding Renaker in 2006.

In the space of nearly two decades, he helped transform Manchester’s skyline and become one of the region’s wealthiest individuals in the process.

He featured in the Sunday Times Rich List earlier this year with a net worth of £698m, up from £140m in 2020.

His rapid rise prompted scrutiny by developers across Manchester, including Mr Weis.

This led Mr Weis to launch legal action last year over the GMCA’s decision to approve £140m loans to Renaker in 2024.

The cash in question was granted to build two city centre skyscrapers.

One, the Contour Tower, will contain a dog spa, a virtual driving range and a Mahjong room, with one-bedroom apartments starting at £275,000.

The Competition Appeal Tribunal ultimately sided with the GMCA in the dispute, which has forged ahead with its latest loans to Renaker. Mr Weis is appealing the decision.

The GMCA claimed last night that it had been made aware of executive changes at Renaker, although it said it would not impact any of its loans with the company.

Filings also show that Mr Whitaker resigned from several UK-based Renaker companies earlier this month.

A GMCA spokesman said: “All loans to the Renaker group have been made to UK-registered companies.

“We have been notified of changes to directorships, but these changes have no effect on the terms of any current loan agreements or the number of new homes they will deliver in Greater Manchester.

“All loan applications were assessed against the same criteria, which were based on parameters set by [the] central overnment and developed with external financial experts to reflect due diligence standards in the private sector.”

Mr Whitaker joins the likes of former Topshop owner Sir Philip Green and easyJet founder Stelios Haji-Ioannou in Monaco, which charges no tax on its residents.

The Renaker founder moved to Manchester in the 1990s to study quantity surveying at the University of Salford.

In a rare interview from 2015, he described towers as the “most efficient way to add vibrancy and mass to a city and accommodate growth”.

He is now closing in on perhaps his most ambitious project yet, as Renaker hopes to build the 71-storey Lighthouse skyscraper consisting of 642 high-end flats.

Featuring a restaurant on the top floor, the building will form one part of a five-tower cluster providing a total of nearly 2,400 homes.

Renaker, which built more than 8,000 homes so far and has another 8,000 in its pipeline, was contacted for comment.

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”

data.title: Best notice accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
If you have a notice account, you’ll be required to notify your bank or building society a specified number of days ahead of making a withdrawal, detailing how much you want to withdraw. This notice period, set by the bank, has to pass before your funds are released.

Notice periods can range from seven days to 180, but the most common notice periods are 30, 60 and 90 days. It’s important to choose an account with a notice period that suits you, as it can be a long wait to get your money.

In theory, your patience is rewarded with higher interest rates than you would get with easy- or instant-access savings accounts. But this isn’t always the case, so you should always do some research before committing to a new account.

In this guide, Telegraph Money addresses:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best notice savings accounts
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account
  • How do notice accounts work?
  • Notice accounts FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best notice savings accounts

Use the table below to discover the best notice savings accounts available.

If you decide you don’t want to wait to withdraw your cash, see our guide to the best easy-access accounts. Opting for one of the best current accounts is another way to earn interest while maintaining full access to your money.

Or, if you’d prefer to lock in a guaranteed rate for the next 12 months or more, don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For a host of tax-efficient savings options, make sure you check our guide to the best cash Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of analysts Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Typically, notice accounts pay a higher rate of interest than easy-access accounts with the benefit of being able to add funds in the account once it’s been opened.

“But an important factor to consider is many providers won’t allow any withdrawals before the end of the notice period and, on the rare occasion earlier access is allowed, may result in a loss of interest.”

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Especially now, when interest rates are declining, notice accounts will typically offer some very attractive rates.

“However, they’re one of the more complicated structures in the savings market. Quite often people don’t realise that they cannot get money out immediately, so they’re saving into a notice account and all of a sudden, they urgently need the money.

“They’re very good for a headline rate, particularly at the moment. But you need to ensure this isn’t money you’re going to need very quickly.”

How do notice accounts work?

Notice accounts work in a very similar way to easy-access accounts; the difference comes when you want to withdraw money.

Instead of requesting a withdrawal that’s paid out instantly, instead you’ll only receive the money after the requisite notice period – as mentioned, this is commonly 30, 60 or 90 days but there are all sorts of options on the market.

Once the notice period is over, your money will be released.

Notice accounts FAQs

Notice savings accounts can be worth it if you can plan your savings withdrawals well in advance. You receive higher interest rates and can usually have unlimited access to your funds – granted, after a bit of a wait.

You can also make ongoing deposits. Unlike some accounts that only accept a single initial deposit, notice accounts allow you to continue adding to your savings whenever you like. Generally, savings will still earn interest during the notice period, but this is worth checking.

For new savers, the notice period may help with money management and preventing impulse buying.

Yes, you can withdraw money from the account, but you’ll need to wait until the notice period passes to receive it. This commonly varies from 30 to 180 days.

Some accounts may impose limits on the number of withdrawals that can be made, while others may have a minimum deposit requirement you’ll need to bear in mind – it is worth checking the account’s terms and conditions to find out.

The biggest disadvantage of a notice account is having to wait for a set amount of time before being able to access your funds, which could be an issue if you need money at short notice.

data.title: North Sea oil giant blames Budget as it slashes jobs
data.description:
The UK’s largest oil and gas producer has announced plans to axe 100 North Sea jobs as a direct result of Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

Harbour Energy said the Chancellor’s refusal to make concessions on the windfall tax meant deep and permanent cuts to its UK workforce were necessary.

The company has already cut 250 onshore office roles this year, after having previously shed 350 workers.

The latest announcement means Harbour has reduced its UK headcount by approximately 700 since 2022, when the windfall tax was introduced by the Conservatives. The levy has pushed overall taxes on oil and gas profits to 78pc till 2030.

Harbour made clear that its decision was directly linked to the Budget. It had held back on redundancies in the hope that Ms Reeves would soften the tax rate, or that Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, would offer a meaningful relaxation of his ban on exploratory drilling.

Scott Barr, the managing director of Harbour’s UK arm, said: “The UK oil and gas sector faces sustained pressure from lower commodity prices and an uncompetitive tax regime, worsened by the Government’s decision to retain the energy profits levy (EPL) in the recent Budget.”

Mr Barr said a redundancy consultation would start immediately and was “necessary to ensure Harbour Energy’s UK business remains competitive as we continue to adapt to a challenging future”.

The announcement comes on the same day that Shell is effectively pulling out of its role as an independent oil and gas operator in the UK North Sea by transferring its assets into Adura, a partnership with Norwegian state-controlled energy giant Equinor.

Mr Barr said: “Harbour’s UK business unit will continue to struggle to compete for capital within our global portfolio while the EPL remains. The future structure of our offshore workforce must adapt to reflect these realities.”

The North Sea industry is already losing around 1,000 jobs a month, according to Offshore Energies UK.

Experts believe unemployment levels will continue to rise. Ashley Kelty, an energy analyst at Panmure Liberum, an investment bank, said: “Labour’s Budget means there is a bucket-load of redundancies yet to come – these are just the first of thousands.”

Harbour operates seven offshore oil and gas platforms in UK waters and has interests in others, collectively producing 156,000 barrels of oil and gas a day.

The windfall levy has left it facing an effective tax rate of 111pc in the UK, according to its latest financial reports.

‘Warnings of job losses are becoming a reality’

Labour has vowed to keep the tax in place until 2030 even though the price of Brent crude has fallen from more than $100 (£75) a barrel when the levy was introduced to just over $60.

Russell Borthwick, the chief executive of Aberdeen and Grampian Chamber of Commerce, said: “The UK Government – by its own admission last week – is now taxing North Sea windfalls which no longer exist.

“The warnings of job losses are becoming a reality – that’s now 700 jobs cut at Harbour Energy alone, plus 400 at Grangemouth, 400 at Mossmoran and thousands more in the wider supply chain.”

Harbour has been refocusing its investments away from the UK. Last year it acquired the upstream operations of Germany’s Wintershall Dea, giving it production centres in Norway, Argentina, Germany and Mexico.

As recently as 2024, UK operations accounted for 80pc of Harbour’s overall output but that has now fallen to just 33pc.

Mr Miliband and his allies argue that an orderly end to oil and gas operations in British waters is necessary for net zero. However, Harbour’s pull-back also poses a threat to one of the country’s key clean energy projects.

Harbour has a central and vital role in the Viking Carbon Capture and Storage project on the Humber on England’s east coast, which aims to capture around 300 million tonnes of CO2.

The company holds a 60pc interest in the transportation and storage infrastructure, of which it would also be the operator. Harbour has made clear that the decline of its UK footprint could put the whole project in danger.

A Government spokesman said: “Our thoughts are with any workers affected by this commercial decision, and we will do everything in our power to support workers and communities.

“This follows restructuring and job losses announced by this company under the previous government.

“This Government has set out a plan to build a prosperous and sustainable future for the North Sea, backed by record investment to grow clean energy industries, support the management of existing oil and gas fields for their lifespan and help North Sea workers make the transition.”

data.title: Revealed: the best savings accounts on the market right now
data.description:
Regardless of why you’re saving – for a house, a holiday, your children’s future or as a buffer to cover emergencies – you’ll want to make sure your money is working as hard as possible.

To help you get the most out of your savings, Telegraph Money has launched a range of “best buy” tables showing the highest rates available across a variety of savings accounts. These tables update automatically every day, with data provided by Savings Data Limited.

It’s more important than ever to make sure your savings are receiving a competitive rate. The Bank of England has cut rates to 4pc, triggering many savings providers to reduce their own rates. Given the latest inflation figure for October measured 3.6pc, it will become increasingly likely that the value of your money will be eroded by rising prices.

Our tables include the best cash Isa rates – whether you want an easy-access, notice, fixed-rate or variable rate account – and we also list the best Lifetime Isas if you’re saving for your first home or retirement.

If you have already maxed out your Isa, or want to save outside of the tax-free wrapper, we track the best easy-access accounts and interest-paying current accounts.

Those who prefer to save monthly should refer to the table of top-paying regular savings accounts, while savers happy to lock their money up for longer in pursuit of higher returns could benefit from our lists of the best fixed-rate bonds and notice accounts.

We also track the best Junior cash Isas and the best children’s savings accounts for parents putting money away for their family’s futures.

In this article, we will cover:

  • The best fixed-rate bonds
  • The best variable savings account rates
  • The best current account rates
  • The best Isa rates
  • The best children’s accounts
  • How to choose the right account for you

Why do rates matter?

Seeking out an account with a more competitive interest rate can make a huge difference.

For example, take a savings account paying a relatively low 0.75pc. A saver with £50,000 in this account will earn around £375 a year in interest. Moving the deposit to an account with a better rate can mean a difference of thousands. Take an account at the higher end offering 4.75pc – you’d yield £2,427 a year, an extra £2,052.

Cash loses value over time if it is left in an account that pays less than the inflation rate. At the current inflation rate of 3.6pc, a pot of £1,000 left in a zero-interest account would be worth just £965.25 after a year in real terms, and just £837.92 after five years. Our inflation calculator can show how your savings are affected.

What is a savings account?

Unlike a current account, which you’ll use to pay your bills and for everyday spending, a savings account is designed to be the place where you can stash your spare money away, where it will hopefully grow over time.

There are several different types of savings accounts – some offer variable rates, which can change at any time, while some require you to commit to locking your cash away for a fixed period.

In theory, the more inconvenient an account is, the more interest you should earn – but it’s not always the case.

The best fixed-rate bonds for 2025

Fixed-rate bonds tend to pay the highest rates on the market. Savers need to weigh up whether to take a fixed deal in order to get guaranteed interest for a longer term, or take a chance on a variable rate that could rise over the length of the term.

The best variable savings account rates for 2025

Variable-rate accounts tend to come with more flexibility than fixed-rate deals; often, you can make as many withdrawals as you like without taking a hit on interest, but some specific deals have their own restrictions.

In general, easy-access accounts let you take out money whenever you like; regular saver accounts require you to make deposits regularly; while notice accounts allow you to make withdrawals, but only if you give your provider a prescribed amount of notice.

By clicking through to each of our guides you can find out today’s best rates.

Easy-access savings account

Regular savings account

Notice savings account

The best current account rates for 2025

In some cases, current accounts will pay interest on the balance of money they hold, which can be useful if you don’t want the hassle of transferring money between multiple accounts.

The best Isa rates for 2025

Cash Isas work in a similar way to savings accounts, except that all interest you earn is tax-free – and you’re restricted to depositing up to £20,000 in each tax year. Income tax is payable on savings interest earned outside of an Isa, above an allowance of £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 in the higher-rate band.

Lifetime Isa

Savers who are under 40 and putting money away to buy a home or fund their retirement can open a Lifetime Isa. As well as the tax-free savings growth, the Government rewards savers with a bonus of 25pc, up to £1,000 a year.

The best children’s accounts

If you’re under the age of 18, or saving on behalf of a youngster, you can open a children’s account. Many account providers will let children add and withdraw money from the age of seven, so it can be a good way to teach financial skills early in life.

Children also get their own Isa allowance of £9,000 a year, which means an adult saving on behalf of a child in a Junior Isa can put this extra amount away on top of their own £20,000 allowance.

What is happening to savings rates?

Hundreds of savings accounts currently offer a rate that beats CPI inflation, which measured 3.6pc in October, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics.

Savings have broadly been on a downward trajectory since they peaked in summer 2023 – but some rates have been falling more quickly this year, since the Bank of England has made several Bank Rate cuts.

The most competitive rates can be quick to disappear – so if you spot an account that looks good, you may need to act fast.

Alice Haine, of online investment platform Bestinvest by Evelyn Partners, says: “For now, the best savings rates are continuing to outstrip inflation, giving savers who hunted out the top deals a healthy real return on their nest eggs. Locking in a top rate now before the best deals disappear could be a sensible strategy for those with cash languishing in an account delivering dismal returns to ensure their money is working as hard as possible.”

Use our calculator to see how much your savings account is earning you – and how much more interest you could get if you switched.

What is the difference between an Isa, bond, savings and a current account?

A current account is a transactional account that typically pays no interest but gives you a lot of flexibility in how often you access your money.

With a savings account, the bank pays you interest for keeping your money and therefore imposes some restrictions on how many withdrawals you can make.

A fixed-rate bond is a savings account with a fixed term, usually between one and five years. Until the duration of the bond is up, you cannot withdraw your funds, but in exchange for the commitment you will typically benefit from a higher rate.

The difference between an Isa and other types of savings account is that there is no tax charged on the interest. Everyone can save up to £20,000 a year tax-free in an Isa, or £9,000 for children.

How to choose the right account for you

The first thing to consider is whether you might need access to the funds in an emergency. A current account or an easy-access savings account will give you this flexibility.

However, you will get a higher rate if you are willing to lock away your funds for a set period (for example, in a bond). Generally, the longer the period, the higher the rate – however, this is not the case at the moment. Fixed-rate accounts with one and two-year terms are far higher than those with five-year rates.

The other thing to consider is whether you are at risk of exceeding your personal savings allowance. This is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. If you earn more than this in interest outside of an Isa, then you will have to pay income tax.

Use our savings tax interest calculator to work out whether you could breach your allowance, and if you should get an Isa.

data.title: Daily Mail owner given three weeks to finalise Telegraph bid
data.description:
The owner of the Daily Mail has been given three weeks to finalise its £500m bid for The Telegraph.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, set the deadline for Lord Rothermere’s holding company DMGT to present its formal plans for consideration, branding the limbo facing this newspaper “unsustainable”.

It has been 19 months since RedBird IMI, a venture mostly backed by the United Arab Emirates, told ministers it would sell The Telegraph after its own bid was blocked over press freedom concerns. The saga began two-and-a-half years ago when the Barclay family lost control to Lloyds Banking Group as a result of their overdue debts.

DMGT announced on Saturday that it had begun exclusive talks towards a £500m deal with RedBird IMI, without giving details of how it intended to structure or fund a transaction.

Ms Nandy’s intervention means the pair must now present their plans to her by mid-December. She said she intended to “build a constructive path toward a timely sale, without further delay, that is in the public interest”.

The Culture Secretary added: “My particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of The Telegraph and its staff, and to the investment appeal of the sector as a whole.”

Lord Rothermere’s company, which owns Metro and The i, as well as the Mail newspaper titles, said it plans to “invest substantially in Telegraph Media Group, with the aim of accelerating its international expansion”.

He has stepped in after previously seeking a 10pc stake in The Telegraph as part of a consortium led by RedBird Capital Partners, the US private equity firm that is the junior partner in RedBird IMI. 

It pulled out this month as it faced continuing concern about its funding, potential foreign state influence and its plans for The Telegraph.

It was the latest in a series of failures by RedBird IMI to exit its investment, which was itself made at pace in late 2023 to derail a competitive auction overseen by Lloyds. 

The UAE-backed venture paid off the Barclay family’s debts and took security over The Telegraph, hoping to convert its position into control.

DMGT has said it will comply with laws on foreign state ownership as there will be “no foreign state investment or capital in the funding structure”.

Ms Nandy warned that any future owner of The Telegraph must be “completely free” of prohibited foreign state influence.

She said she would also exercise her powers to uphold the public interest, which in newspaper mergers surrounds accurate news, free expression of opinion and plurality.

Lord Rothermere and RedBird IMI will require Ms Nandy’s permission for any transaction to proceed, and are likely to face investigation by Ofcom and potentially the Competition and Markets Authority of its impact on media plurality in the UK.

The combination of The Telegraph and DMGT would account for about half the newspaper market.

Lord Rothermere is expected to argue that the plurality of online news sources and tech distribution platforms would guard against an unhealthy concentration of influence. It has been speculated that he could sell The i and Metro to help assuage some of the concerns.

The 57-year-old, whose family fortune was founded in the 19th century newspaper boom, also said he would maintain The Telegraph’s editorial independence from the Daily Mail and invest in international growth.

Plurality debates are likely to focus on the effect that ownership of The Telegraph and the Daily Mail under one umbrella might have. 

DMGT’s 2019 takeover of The i was waved through by Ofcom partly because the newspaper was found to offer a different viewpoint to the Daily Mail.

Ms Nandy said that The Telegraph’s editorial independence must continue to be protected until its ownership is resolved.

data.title: Best easy-access accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
In their purest form, easy-access accounts provide straightforward access to your cash. They can be the simplest type of savings account to hold, and are a good idea if you want to regularly deposit and withdraw funds.

Easy-access accounts usually have variable interest rates, which can be increased and decreased at the provider’s discretion – but it should always let you know before the rate changes.

However, these accounts can come with a range of terms that make them more complicated. For instance, some include introductory bonus rates, which are essentially temporary offers to attract new customers. They’re usually good deals when you first sign up, but you’ll need to keep an eye on when they end and might want to switch as soon as they do.

Some accounts also have limited access – for instance, only allowing a certain number of withdrawals each year. If you exceed these, you may be punished with a lower interest rate or the account may automatically close. It’s worth checking the terms to make sure you’re clear on how the account works before you sign up.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best easy-access savings accounts for December 2025
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account
  • Variable interest vs fixed-rate
  • Easy access account FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, such as those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS), which covers up to £85,000 per person, per financial institution. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

Best easy-access savings accounts for December 2025

See the table below to see the current top-rate easy-access savings accounts.

There are lots of other accounts that will let you access your cash whenever you want to. See our guides to the best current accounts that pay interest, the best regular accounts and best notice accounts – not to mention our guide to the best cash Isas, which lists out a range of the top tax-free accounts.

For options on the top rates when you lock your cash away for a year or more, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Easy-access accounts are useful for keeping an emergency fund to cover any unexpected costs, as they allow savers to dip in and out when needed.”

But there are downsides, she added: “As the money isn’t locked away, it may be more tempting for savers to unnecessarily withdraw cash.

“The interest rates on easy-access accounts are variable, so could drop at short notice. In some cases, the number of withdrawals may be limited and, if exceeded, may cause the rate to drop dramatically.”

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, added: “Easy-access accounts are probably the most popular in the market at the moment, offering good rates.

“However, clients do need to be aware that this rate is not guaranteed. It is important to monitor the easy-access product as quite often clients forget and then a year later, realise the rate is not attractive.”

Variable interest vs fixed-rate

Easy access accounts are almost always variable, which means the interest rate can change at any time. Fixed-rate accounts, as the name suggests, keep the same rate for the duration of the fixed term.

Both have pros and cons, depending on what’s happening with the savings market. Signing up to a fixed rate can shield you in times where rates are on the way down and, to compensate for the fact that most of these accounts don’t allow withdrawals during the fixed term, the rates they offer are usually higher.

However, in times when savings rates are rising, having your cash stuck in a fixed rate account can mean you’ll miss out on higher interest elsewhere.

Easy access account FAQs

While instant-access and easy-access accounts are very similar, the slight difference is that instant-access accounts allow you to make withdrawals directly from the account, with no limit on how much you can withdraw or when you can take money out.

An easy-access account can include some restrictions, such as those we’ve mentioned above. Easy-access accounts might also have daily withdrawal limits and you might need to transfer the cash to a current account before you can actually spend it.

It’s generally accepted that you should have between three and six months’ worth of living expenses saved to fall back on in an emergency. If you’re retired, this increases to one to three years’ worth. It’s good to hold this money in an easy-access account that allows you to withdraw quickly, whenever you might need it.

If you have larger sums that you don’t need access to, you might find a better rate by putting it in a fixed-rate bond, or you might consider investing.

The minimum balance for an easy-access account can vary hugely, and it’s not uncommon to see accounts requiring anything from as little as £1 up to £10,000. Shop around for the account which best suits your needs and budget.

data.title: Best fixed-rate bonds: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Fixed-rate bonds are savings accounts that lock away your funds for a set period of time, paying a guaranteed set rate of interest throughout the term.

They commonly span from nine months to five years and, in theory, the longer the term you commit to, the higher the interest rate you receive. However, this is not always the case – during times when rates are expected to fall over the longer term, savings providers will increase rates for shorter-term bonds in a bid to avoid paying over the odds in future.

If you need to make a withdrawal before the end of the term, you may be unable to – providers that do allow early access may impose a penalty by removing some of the interest you have earned thus far, so be certain you do not need the funds before opening your account.

You will often need to pay in a lump sum at the start of the term, rather than making regular deposits. The interest can be paid annually, quarterly or monthly, depending on your provider. In some cases, interest may only be paid at the end of the term – if you have a large amount saved over a number of years, this could mean you will exceed your personal savings allowance and have to pay tax on your returns.

If your provider is covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme, then your money is protected up to £85,000 per person, per financial institution should your provider go bust.

This guide will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best fixed-rate bonds
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond
  • Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds
  • Fixed-rate bond FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update every day.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best fixed-rate bonds

Use the table below to explore your options for a fixed-rate deal.

For the top rates where you can access your cash whenever you like, see our guide to the best easy access accounts and the best current accounts.

Alternatively, our guide to the best notice accounts can show you the top accounts that require you to wait a little while before your money is released.

While you’re saving, you might be keen to shield your money from a potential tax bill – our guide to the best cash Isas can help you find a tax-efficient account.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond

Rachel Springall, of the analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Picking the right fixed bond term entirely depends on someone’s circumstances and how soon they wish to access their pot. There are bonds with fixed terms of just a few months, so these might be useful for someone who has a big lump sum but may need to use it before the year is over, such as a deposit for a home.

“There are fixed bonds available that pay interest monthly, which may be suited to customers looking to supplement their income.”

She also warned customers to consider the personal savings allowance (PSA) when it came to accounts that were not Isas, as there could be tax to pay on the interest you earn.

Fixed-rate bonds are worth considering at the moment, due to expected falls in the Bank of England’s Bank Rate, which tend to encourage savings providers to reduce rates.

“Interest rates are expected to fall further in the coming months, so some savers might want a longer-term fixed bond to secure a guaranteed return for longer. Some fixed bonds require large sums upfront and most fixed accounts do not allow early access, so it’s worth taking time to compare deals carefully before investing.

“As it stands, some of the top rates available are from challenger banks, which should not be overlooked,” Ms Springall added.

Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds

While Premium Bonds and fixed-rate bonds might sound similar, they are very different:

  • Premium Bonds are only provided by National Savings & Investments (NS&I), while fixed-rate bonds are offered by a number of savings providers.
  • Premium Bonds aren’t savings accounts; you don’t get paid interest. Instead, each £1 bond is entered into a monthly prize draw, where you could win between £25-£1m. Fixed-rate bonds pay a set amount of interest for the duration of the term.
  • Premium Bonds prizes are paid tax-free; savings interest from fixed-rate bonds is taxable unless it’s a fixed-rate cash Isa.

Fixed-rate bond FAQS

You might have to pay tax on the interest your fixed-rate bond earns if it exceeds your tax-free allowance, unlike the money stored in an Isa.

This allowance depends on your tax bracket; basic-rate taxpayers can earn £1,000 in interest tax-free but the limit is £500 for higher-rate taxpayers and no personal savings allowance for additional-rate taxpayers.

Those with incomes of less than £17,570 may be able to benefit from the additional starting rate for savings, which gives those on lower incomes up to £5,000 as an extra tax-free savings allowance.

If you usually pay tax by self-assessment, you will be asked to detail the amount of savings interest you have earned over the tax year. If you pay tax via Pay As You Earn (PAYE), any tax on savings interest is usually calculated automatically and your tax code may be adjusted if you have tax to pay.

You must submit a tax return if you earn more than £10,000 in savings interest – regardless of whether or not you usually file one.

This will depend on what rates are available at the time you are looking for a bond, as well as whether the account suits your circumstances.

The best way to determine how “good” an interest rate is, is to see how it compares with other similar types of bond on the market. If it can easily be beaten by other providers, it might be worth looking elsewhere.

Our table of the best fixed-rate bonds above shows some of the top deals currently available.

NS&I’s Premium Bonds are not fixed-rate bonds – and they work slightly differently to other types of savings accounts.

They do not pay any interest, instead every £1 you have invested is assigned its own Premium Bonds number, which is entered into a monthly prize draw, whether you could win between £25 and £1m. Our guide to Premium Bonds explains how they work in more detail.

NS&I does offer fixed-rate bonds, including Guaranteed Growth Bonds and Guaranteed Income Bonds – see our guide to NS&I savings accounts to find out more.

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: BBC licence fee to approach £200 a year
data.description:
The cost of the BBC licence fee will surge to almost £200 after the next election as a further one million households stop paying the levy.

Official forecasts from the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) show the licence fee will rise from its current rate of £174.50 to just under £197 by the end of the decade if it continues to rise in line with inflation.

Meanwhile, the number of licence fee payers is expected to fall by more than one million to 21 million.

It comes after new figures revealed the BBC lost more than £1bn from licence fee evasion and cancellation last year.

A further 300,000 households stopped paying the levy in 2024 as audiences increasingly shun the BBC in favour of streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney.

Overall, 3.6 million now say they do not have a licence because they do not need one, according to a report by the public accounts committee. Meanwhile, one in eight users now evade payment despite using the BBC’s services.

The figures underscore the crisis facing the BBC as it heads into negotiations over its future funding model amid a crisis over bias in its reporting.

The public service broadcaster is in disarray after The Telegraph published a dossier exposing how bosses had failed to address internal concerns about bias, including a Panorama documentary that misleadingly edited Donald Trump’s speech on the day of the Capitol riots.

The controversy led to the resignation of Tim Davie as director general and Deborah Turness as the broadcaster’s head of news. Samir Shah, the BBC chairman, who was grilled by MPs this week over his handling of the crisis, is also under scrutiny.

The scandal has raised questions over the BBC’s future as it enters into negotiations with the Government about the renewal of its Royal Charter, which expires in 2027.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she will consider all options including reform of the licence fee, though she has insisted ministers will ensure the corporation has sustainable funding.

Nevertheless, Ms Nandy is currently considering whether to block an increase in the licence fee by almost £7 to over £180 in the spring as households are already struggling with rising food and energy bills.

The Culture Secretary must decide by February whether to allow the inflation-linked rise to go ahead as planned.

Ministers have previously intervened in the licence fee. In 2022, the Conservative government froze the levy for two years, after which it was agreed that the fee would rise in line with inflation for the remainder of the Charter period.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 as a result of the squeeze on its funding.

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn. It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In last week’s Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

Your Isa allowance is renewed at the start of the tax year on April 6, and you cannot carry over any unused allowance from the previous year – if you don’t use it, you lose it.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in December
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in December

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These types of Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your returns remain free from tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you increasingly need to shop around for them as rates continue to slide. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, you may miss the end of the tax year if you leave it too late to make a deposit.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can put all of this into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Britain threatened by gas shortages as North Sea output plummets
data.description:
Britain faces the risk of severe gas shortages as plummeting North Sea production leaves the country dangerously reliant on imports, the UK grid operator has warned.

In a report published on Wednesday, the National Energy System Operator (Neso) said gas production was poised for a steep decline because of Labour’s energy policies – posing a risk to Britain’s energy security as the grid becomes in hock to foreign suppliers.

Neso’s analysis came after it assessed the impact of Labour’s ban on new exploration and the 78pc tax rates imposed on North Sea gas producers.

According to the energy body, gas availability will be 78pc lower by 2035 compared with today – equivalent to a decline of up to 13pc a year.

It also warned that the amount of gas produced by the North Sea this year will decline by over 80pc – from 27 billion cubic metres (bcm) to just 5bcm by 2035 – making the UK massively reliant on supplies from Norway, the US and Qatar.

“During periods of peak demand in the 2030s, we expect GB’s import dependency could rise above 90pc,” said the report.

“Our analysis identifies an emerging risk to UK gas supply security. In scenarios where all gas supply and network infrastructure are operational, this risk is evident only where peak gas demand remains close to or above current expectations.

“However, in the unlikely event of the loss of the single largest piece of gas infrastructure, gas supply falls short of demand.”

The reference to gas infrastructure refers to the potential failure of the pipelines feeding Norwegian gas to the UK or to a liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminal going offline.

Norway supplies nearly half the UK’s gas via North Sea pipelines – but it too is running out, with production set to halve between now and 2035, Neso warned.

The findings contradict Ed Miliband’s pledge to make the UK less reliant on imports and on “petrostates”.

In September, the Energy Secretary told the Labour Party conference: “We’re at the mercy of the petrostates and dictators. And the only way out of it is our mission for clean power by 2030.”

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “This report should be a wake-up call. It shows that the UK is in the midst of a gas supply crisis made in Whitehall by Ed Miliband and Rachel Reeves, who have created an unprecedented, policy-driven decline of North Sea gas production.”

Chris Wheaton, an analyst at Stifel, said Neso could even be downplaying the threat: “We think Neso forecasts are too optimistic. We forecast gas production to decline by 70pc by 2030 due to the impact of the windfall tax.

“That suggests the UK will run out of LNG import capacity as early as 2031, as the current import terminals reach the maximum rate they can deliver gas into the UK grid during winter. The best way to ensure security of gas supply is to maximise domestic gas production.”

The Neso report points out that gas demand surges in winter and can hit peaks of 482 million cubic metres of gas per day (mcm/day). Even by 2035 when decarbonisation will have been long under way, it predicts winter demand hitting 422mcm/day.

But by then, North Sea contribution will have fallen to about a fifth of what will be needed just for the UK’s gas-fired power stations. In Britain, 27 million homes rely on gas boilers for heat and hot water.

Ashley Kelty, from Panmure Liberum, said: “Current government policy makes no sense. Its energy policy is driven by misguided and ignorant net zero dogma which places more importance on virtue signalling than energy security.

“We saw the dangers of over-reliance on imports when Russia invaded Ukraine – this should be a lesson to move to rely on domestic supplies rather than imports.”

Richard Tice, Reform UK’s energy spokesman, said Britain’s plight was caused by poor policy rather than geology: “We need to increase UK oil and gas exploration by reducing the energy profits levy and motivating investment with smarter regulation removing lots of unnecessary rules.”

data.title: A shortage of seafarers threatens Trump’s fossil fuel boom
data.description:
Donald Trump’s attempt to double US exports of liquefied natural gas (LNG) within five years faces an unexpected threat: the waning popularity of life at sea.

The US president plans to make LNG production the cornerstone of a new fossil fuel boom, symbolised by his promise to “drill, baby, drill”. To get production pumping, Trump wants to flood the world with American LNG – a fuel Western countries have increasingly come to rely on in the wake of Russia’s war in Ukraine.

His plan is spurring demand for hundreds of new ships over the next decade, needed to transport the liquefied fuel. Yet a lack of seafarers to man the vast new fleet may render that ambition an impossibility.

The shipping industry is in the grip of a recruitment crisis as nations that have traditionally supplied crews to merchant vessels see their young populations increasingly turn away from the ocean for more comfortable employment ashore.

One senior oil executive says recruitment of LNG crews may be one of the biggest obstacles facing Trump’s planned energy boom.

They are not the only one who is concerned. “It’s not attractive for people in developed countries – and in particular Gen Z – to spend such a long time away from family and friends, whatever the pay,” says Martin Roolvink, the managing director at Pronav, which provides more than 500 crew for LNG carriers.

Roolvink says the majority of officers on Pronav’s books are Croatian or Ukrainian – but even in those places, becoming a seafarer “is not that popular any more”.

Work-life balance is an issue since LNG terminals, which store and convert the fuel, are usually located far from population centres. Ships’ crews, meanwhile, have little opportunity to visit towns and “probably will not see anything shoreside for three months”, Roolvink says.

Compounding the problem is the fact that LNG – comprised mostly of frozen methane – is among the most dangerous of cargoes, representing both a fire risk and a threat to the vessels that carry it, given the risk of cold-induced hull fractures.

The huge storage tanks on LNG vessels also give them a high “windage area”, which makes them harder to handle in stormy conditions and high seas, according to Shyam Paliwal, founder of Tank Terminal Training.

Sailors seeking to move into the sector from traditional cargo vessels – such as container ships, bulk carriers and even oil tankers – must gain extensive qualifications and undergo years of on-the-job training.

Roolvink says: “LNG carriers are much more complex than bulk or container vessels. They have specific technical features – including very sophisticated systems for automation, cargo containment, tank systems and propulsion – which require highly trained personnel.

“Loading and discharging are critical operations. Once you are connected to the shore terminal, there is a huge amount of attention required with 24/7 watches.”

Firms have resorted to poaching seasoned seafarers from liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) carriers, the nearest thing to LNG vessels but without the ultra-high pressure, -160 degrees Celsius storage.

Throwing money at the problem, though, has pushed labour costs towards unsustainable levels. Experienced LNG ship masters command as much as $20,000 (£15,000) a month to captain vessels on short-term contracts.

The hiring squeeze is likely to persist as more vessels hit the water. The number of ships designed to transport LNG currently stands at around 750 but is forecast to surpass the 900-strong global oil tanker fleet for the first time next year.

Shipping broker Clarksons reckons 451 new LNG carriers worth a total of $103bn will be ordered this decade – though producing the ships will itself be a challenge with barely half a dozen shipyards capable of constructing them, mostly in South Korea.

Global exports are forecast to reach record levels next year and it’s expected that trade volumes will surge 60pc by 2030, making LNG a mainstay of global energy consumption.

Exports will be led by the US and Qatar, which are expected to control 50pc of LNG supply by the end of the decade, with European and Asian nations the biggest importers.

Evidence of the boom can be seen in the rapidly rising cost of transporting LNG. Carriage rates hit $170,000 per day on the London market this month, the highest for two years (rates reached an eye-watering $400,000 a day in the immediate wake of the Ukraine invasion).

The surge in demand could translate into a requirement for as many as 18,000 LNG-qualified seafarers, according to some forecasts.

Roolvink reckons the sector will need to find at least 3,500 senior officers alone if it is to keep pace with anticipated rates of growth. He says: “It’s one of the biggest concerns of the sector and it means planning is essential.

“The companies involved must ensure that the people are trained and that they have a long-term perspective, otherwise they will not be able to man the ships.”

LNG has boomed in popularity partly because of its versatility. While LNG ships are much more high-spec than other vessels, the fuel is still easier to transport than natural gas, which can only be moved over long distances via pipelines.

It has also been enthusiastically embraced by governments as a “transition fuel”: it produces less carbon dioxide than coal or oil and provides a bridge to future reliance on renewables.

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022 spurred a surge in demand, with LNG credited with keeping the lights on in Europe as Moscow slashed traditional gas supplies.

Now Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” approach to fossil fuels is set to take supply to new highs.

The president signed an executive order bearing the title Unleashing American Energy on his inauguration day, overturning a ban on new applications for LNG exports imposed by Joe Biden in 2024 amid pressure from climate activists.

The US only began selling LNG overseas in 2016 but had become the world’s largest exporter by 2023, prior to the Biden moratorium.

Trump’s intervention has paved the way for up to 20 new LNG terminals – the facilities for storing and converting the fuel – in the US, in addition to the eight that are fully operational and three already under construction.

Whether there are enough people to staff them remains to be seen.
