data.title: Why science is racing to reinvent chocolate
data.description:
Something’s wrong in Wonka Land. Chocolate bars are shrinking. Recipes are changing. Levels in the chocolate river are running low, and the very word “chocolate” is disappearing from wrappers. A sinister Roald Dahl twist? Sadly not. Cocoa is in crisis.

The defining ingredient in the world’s favourite sweet treats, grown only in a narrow band around the Equator, is taking a battering. Disease, climate change, years of underinvestment and falling production have sent prices soaring. Between late 2022 and 2024, the price of cocoa surged more than threefold from $2,367 (£1,790) per ton to a 45-year high of $11,984, according to the International Cocoa Organisation. Prices are easing for now, but cocoa still costs more than twice what it did in 2020. And it’s all taking a toll on the chocolate aisle.

Faced with huge costs, manufacturers are reformulating, swapping some of the cocoa in firm favourites for cheaper substitutes. As a result, some chocolate coatings have been officially downgraded to “chocolate-flavoured”. McVitie’s Penguin and Club bars, along with KitKat White and McVitie’s White Digestives, no longer meet the legal threshold to call themselves chocolate at all.

“Shrinkflation” is also biting hard. Mondelez International, which owns Cadbury, says it has reduced the size of some products as a “last resort” to keep them affordable; across the industry, bars and packets are downsizing while prices stay much the same. That Quality Street tin you’re looking forward to this Christmas? There will be less to squabble over because it’s roughly five chocs lighter.

But as the chocolate giants scramble to adapt, a new wave of scientists and entrepreneurs is racing to reimagine chocolate, replacing cocoa beans with unlikely raw materials, and keeping their methods firmly under wraps. It’s hardly surprising: with the global chocolate market worth more than $130bn (£100bn), a credible stand-in for cocoa would be close to striking gold. The question is whether anything can truly match the flavour, texture and deep nostalgia bound up in real chocolate, the kind made with cocoa.

Win-Win believes it is pretty close. The London-based firm makes cocoa-free chocolate from grains and legumes, including rice, fermented using a secret process. “By pairing this breakthrough technology with classic chocolate-making techniques like roasting, grinding, refining and tempering, we’ve unlocked a chocolate experience that is indistinguishable from conventional products, but with a dramatically lower environmental footprint,” says chief executive Mark Golder.

He’s not predicting the end of real chocolate, thankfully. “At the heart of the ongoing cocoa crisis is a fundamental imbalance – the world wants more chocolate than the planet and the current supply chain can provide,” Golder says. Chocolate alternatives can help close that gap, especially when used in mass-produced foods such as cakes, biscuits, ice cream and confectionery. “For some small businesses, the cost of cocoa can be the difference between surviving or not,” he adds.

German start-up Planet A Foods has developed an alternative made from sunflower seeds, using a fermentation process based on beer brewing. Like most cocoa-free chocolate, ChoViva is not only cheaper than the real thing but kinder to the planet, using less water and producing fewer carbon emissions while still tasting reasonably authentic.

What began as a kitchen experiment by food technologist Sara Marquardt and her brother Max during lockdown in 2020, ChoViva, is now a brand in its own right turning over tens of millions of euros a year, with 73 products available in eight countries. In the UK, it features in Aldi’s Dairyfine range and Candy Kittens’ vegan sweets. The company recently signed a long-term deal with manufacturer Barry Callebaut, which supplies chocolate to culinary professionals globally, which will vastly expand its reach.

Sunflower seeds, Marquardt says, offer an ideal foundation for a chocolate alternative: they share key biochemical “building blocks” with cocoa, which means that with the right processing they can be coaxed into something convincingly chocolate-like.

They are also abundant, drought resistant and grown close to home – factors that slash ChoViva’s carbon footprint by around 80 per cent compared with conventional chocolate. “Sunflower seeds are also very affordable,” she notes. And despite the turbulence caused by the war in Ukraine – historically one of the world’s major suppliers – there’s still no shortage of seeds to keep the confectionery experiments going.

ChoViva is available as a powder and a meltable “chocolate” alternative for manufacturers to use in everything from ice cream coatings to chocolate bars. There’s a cocoa butter version in development, too, but EU red tape has slowed its progress to market: classed as a “novel food”, it can’t be sold until it clears a lengthy regulatory process.

Unlike rivals whose chocolate alternatives are designed to be used as “extenders” – a partial replacement for cocoa – ChoViva is intended as a complete like-for-like substitute, to help ease pressure on real chocolate supplies. “We believe strongly in our mission to have chocolate available to our kids and grandchildren in the future,” says Marquardt. “What drives us is making ingredients like this accessible and affordable for everyone.”

Italian company Foreverland is taking a different tack again, building its Choruba “chocolate” from waste products: carob pulp, grape seeds discarded by the wine industry, and a by-product of processed pumpkin seeds.

Carob has long been grown across the Mediterranean and used in sweets and syrups, but these days its seeds are prized by the ice cream industry, leaving the pulp as a plentiful by-product. Outside the region, carob is probably best remembered as the supposedly healthy – and for some of us, distinctly weird tasting – chocolate substitute of the 1970s. But Riccardo Bottiroli, Foreverland’s co-founder, says its secret fermentation and roasting process removes the carob flavour and results in a chocolatey and eco-friendly cocoa alternative.

“Carob doesn’t require any water other than rainfall to grow,” he says. “It’s drought resistant and widely available across the region.” Foreverland’s “chocolate” pieces and powders are now finding favour among manufacturers in Italy, Germany and France who see the appeal of a product made close to home from crops that demand little of the planet.

Britain is set to join the cocoa-free chocolate race, too. From its lab and test kitchen in Guildford, Nukoko is turning humble fava beans grown in the UK and Europe into something that looks, melts and tastes like chocolate, according to the company. Co-founder Ross Newton, who previously ran a chocolate company with fellow founder Kit Tomlinson, says the idea grew out of sheer frustration with volatile cocoa prices.

Together with plant biologist Prof David Salt, they began experimenting. “We tried hundreds of different types of legumes,” Newton says. “We needed a bean that was abundant locally so we didn’t have to transport it from the other side of the world.” They landed on fava beans, which have the added advantage of enriching the soil they are cultivated in, adding nitrogen. Once the beans pass through Nukoko’s closely guarded fermentation process, they are dried, roasted and ground just as cocoa beans are, into a fine powder that tastes nothing like fava beans but carries some of the layered flavour of chocolate.

How is it possible to mimic the flavour of cocoa, a complex bean containing more than a thousand compounds? Prof Salt explains that only a small fraction of them actually drive flavour. “There’s good literature where people have recreated the odour and taste of cocoa with 20 pure compounds,” he says. With the right processes, including fermentation and roasting, those key compounds can be developed in raw materials that aren’t cocoa at all. Many cocoa-free chocolates also contain natural flavours, such as vanilla, to round out the taste.

The first batches of Nukoko, due to reach the market early in the new year, will go to food manufacturers. “Lots of companies aren’t looking to completely replace cocoa,” Newton says. “They want to reduce their cocoa usage – for example, use 50 per cent Nukoko and 50 per cent cocoa.”

While Nukoko focuses on beans, Belfast-based biotechnologist Azhar Murtaza, the founder of food tech company Born Maverick, has looked to fruit for inspiration. His chocolate substitute, Betta Choc, is made with date seeds, an abundant by-product of one of the world’s oldest crops. He believes that his product has the edge over competitors because not only is it is naturally sweet and contains no added natural flavours, but it also contains the feel-good compound theobromine.

Murtaza’s method involves fermenting date seeds with a strain of mycelium, a fungus that quickly breaks them down, triggering chemical reactions that develop cocoa-like flavours. It’s also when theobromine – the compound that gives chocolate its subtle mood-lifting, energising effect – develops. “People eat chocolate because it makes them feel good, and that comes from theobromine,” Murtaza says. “It’s not added chemically; it’s done naturally within the fermentation process.”

He says powdered Betta Choc has been trialled by a chocolate multinational (he can’t reveal which one), for use as a replacement for 70-80 per cent of the cocoa in a well-known bar. “They loved it,” he says of the company, which offered to collaborate. He politely declined and is now in talks with the government of Saudi Arabia, where there’s no shortage of date seeds, in the hope of securing investment to set up production there.

Murtaza has deliberately designed his process to be low-cost, unlike some ventures in the United States and elsewhere now trying to grow cocoa in labs from scratch, in the same vein as cultured meat. “That’s hugely expensive,” he says. “How are they going to bring down the cost of that for mass consumers?”

Others disagree, arguing that this is the next chocolate frontier. In California, start-ups such as California Cultured are working to grow cocoa by reproducing cells from real beans in bioreactors. The idea is to create cocoa products with the same molecular structure as the genuine article, but in weeks rather than years, and without the diseases and droughts threatening tropical plantations. Similar work is under way in Israel at Celleste Bio, which calls its facilities “indoor cocoa farms”.

At the same time, big chocolate manufacturers aren’t giving up on cocoa altogether. In West Africa, where more than 60 per cent of the world’s supply is grown, scientists backed by Mars Inc and researchers at the University of California, Davis are racing to develop plants that can withstand soaring temperatures and disease. The goal is to future-proof cocoa by making it more resilient and less reliant on the shrinking regions where it can survive.

This is all part of a growing recognition that the era of cheap, abundant cocoa might be drawing to a close, and that the future of chocolate will hinge on science and innovation. But it might be years before lab-grown cocoa and climate-hardy trees can produce affordable beans on the scale needed to feed the world’s appetite for chocolate. In the meantime, as the new generation of producers is proving, crops grown far from the tropics can stand in for cocoa so convincingly that many consumers won’t notice the difference. And some chefs are embracing it, too.

Chantelle Nicholson, the founder of sustainable London restaurant Apricity, is a fan of Win-Win’s cocoa-free chocolate. “It’s a delicious ingredient and is very comparable to chocolate,” she says. The fruity notes work beautifully in fruit-based desserts, and the white version is “on par” with good white chocolate, she adds.

I tasted confectionery and biscuit samples coated in cocoa-free chocolate from most of the companies I spoke to for this article. The “chocolate” looked the part and, to my surprise, melted much like the real thing. Although they tasted pleasant, like chocolate confectionery, any trace of cocoa flavour was overwhelmed by the sweetness, caramel, biscuit or nuts, and they ended up tasting more or less the same. The exception was Win-Win, whose white, milk and dark “chocolate” buttons had a more complex, more convincing flavour.

Chocolate expert and Academy of Chocolate judge Jennifer Earle says most consumers won’t notice if the cocoa in their favourite confectionery bar is replaced with one of these pretenders. “They’re not bad at approximating the chocolate-flavoured coating on cheap sweets, but they still don’t capture the complexity of good-quality chocolate,” she says. “Mass-market confectionery has relied on heavy processing and added flavourings for years. The variety of flavours within cocoa has been largely engineered out.”

Earle believes that chocolate alternatives have their place in a world where demand outstrips supply, especially for those brands with a genuinely smaller environmental impact. The fear is that big chocolate companies will give up on cocoa-based chocolate altogether. “It would be infuriating to see that instead of paying cocoa farmers properly to continue to grow what they’ve dedicated their land to, chocolate companies abandon them to get a cheaper, non-cocoa alternative,” she says.

One deterrent to turning their backs on cocoa is the word itself: “chocolate” resonates deeply on a nostalgic, emotional, sensory and taste level. And while cocoa-free bars might look and melt like the real thing, legally they can’t be called chocolate. UK labelling rules reserve that word for products made with defined amounts of cocoa solids and cocoa butter, so anything built from other ingredients – however clever the science – has to find another name.

In Earle’s view, there is room for both real chocolate and cocoa-free alternatives. But for cocoa to survive, farmers need support to face the pressures of climate change and the many other problems besetting their industry. And that means that chocolate lovers must be prepared to pay more for the good stuff. “Real chocolate that’s made with beans that are economically, environmentally and socially well sourced already should cost at least £8 per 100g, and that does make it a pricey treat for most people,” Earle says. But if enough of that were passed on to farmers to invest in climate-change-resistant, more productive plants, cocoa’s future could be secure.

In other words, cocoa-free chocolate may help ease the strain as prices soar and global demand outstrips supply. Consumers may not notice something has changed in their favourite bar. But for true chocolate lovers, there’s no substitute for the rich, joyful complexity of quality chocolate made with cocoa. Perhaps the real Wonka magic will lie in keeping the real thing alive too, not just coming up with a replacement.

How do they taste?

Jennifer Earle blind-tasted four cocoa-free alternatives, and gives her verdict in order of preference.

Choruba milk ‘chocolate’

Looks and snaps like supermarket milk chocolate, but its aroma is pure carob. The texture mimics chocolate, though it’s slightly drier and sandy. Far sweeter than craft milk chocolate, the flavour is dominated by sugar and a muted carob-cereal note. Pleasant enough for those who like very sweet confectionery, but the sugary cereal-milk aftertaste doesn’t deliver a true chocolate experience.

Where to try: not yet available in the UK. Used in confectionery and baked goods in Italy, and soon to expand into Germany, France and Nordic countries.

Win-Win milk ‘chocolate’

Looks much like milk chocolate, though the snap is slightly weak and dull. The aroma is mostly sugar with a clear carob note. The flavour is sweet carob softened by milk, vanilla and, possibly, other grains that take the edge off the carob. The melt is impressively close to real chocolate, and it’s far better than a straight carob bar, but it still doesn’t taste like chocolate.

Where to try: used in British bakeries and restaurants (including Apricity in London). Will be available direct to the public next year.

Betta Choc filled ‘chocolate’

Looks like chocolate, though the sheen is a little dull. The aroma is mostly carob with a touch of vanilla that could, at a glance, pass for basic supermarket chocolate. Tastes firmly of carob with a texture close enough to be convincing – an impressive effort for something with no cocoa at all. Lacks the depth of real chocolate, though if you’d grown up with it, the sugar-fat hit might be enough.

Where to try: not yet available.

ChoViva filled milk ‘chocolate’

The texture does not approximate chocolate, as it’s sandy and melts too quickly. Incredibly sweet and tastes of rice and vanilla, with earthy notes. Tastes cheap and quite unpleasant. It’s really not anything like chocolate except in colour and the fact that both contain sugar and fat.

Where to try: in the UK, ChoViva is available in Aldi’s Dairyfine range and Candy Kittens’ vegan sweets.

Nukoko

Not sampled and not yet available.

data.title: High streets emerge as the biggest losers of Reeves’s shock property tax shake-up
data.description:
When retail boss John Jones heard Rachel Reeves deliver her Budget last week, he was pleasantly surprised by the Chancellor’s upbeat tone.

Around 750,000 small stores, pubs, restaurants and bars would be better off from her changes to business rates, she suggested, which was music to the ears of retail chiefs like Jones who have faced an annus horribilis after the previous Budget.

“She was speaking about supporting the high street, and so my first thought was, ‘This sounds alright’,” says Jones, who runs his family department store Philip Morris & Sons.

But by the evening he was feeling noticeably less cheery.

The Chancellor’s shake-up of business rates had left his family’s department store facing a sharp rise in its property tax bill.

Across its three sites, the department store’s business rates bill will rise by 45pc to the highest level it has ever been. “It’s not exactly support for the high street,” says Jones.

Up and down the country, business chiefs have spent the past few days reeling from the Chancellor’s business rates shake-up, which had promised to level the playing field “between the high street and online giants” but has stealthily delivered a hammer blow to small firms.

In a nutshell, changing how property bills were calculated was supposed to save the high street. Critics said the current system made it more attractive to run an online store, so the Government proposed a new calculation.

But for some, the cure may be worse than the problem.

According to tax firm Ryan, the overhaul will pile more pressure on high street businesses, while offering tax cuts to obscure properties including windmills, public telephone boxes and ATMs.

Around 3,480 retail properties will pay an extra £112m in rates from April, according to Ryan’s analysis, both because the sites have gone up in so-called “rateable value” and also because Covid business rates relief has been cut.

The Treasury claims that it has offset this by setting a lower multiplier, with the overall tax rate down.

But the British Independent Retailers Association (Bira) estimates that, even with transitional reliefs and the Government’s new lower “multipliers” used to calculate business rates, a typical independent retailer will see their bill rise by 15pc next year.

Businesses feel ‘shafted’

Other high street businesses are facing similar pressure.

According to Ryan, around 5,370 retail, leisure and hospitality premises are in line to pay £182m extra in business rates from next year. Hotels, holiday parks and music venues are also expected to be the biggest losers from the changes.

Shaun Whitehouse, a hotelier who runs the Lanes Hotel in Yeovil, said many in his industry feel they have been misled by Reeves.

“The Chancellor presented the changes as something positive but once you read the detail it becomes clear that it’s anything but,” he said.

“Most of us are on the brink. There are business owners who say they didn’t know if they had weeks or even days left. What’s really sad is that many people have now accepted there is no future for their businesses – and it is entirely down to taxation.”

Whitbread, which owns the Premier Inn chain, last week said it was bracing for a £50m hit to annual profits. Dominic Paul, chief executive, said he was “extremely disappointed” with the Budget.

Clive Watson, the boss of the City Pub Company, also said pubs and restaurants have been “shafted by this Government with their tax-grabbing measures”.

Even though the Government is reducing the “multiplier” to determine business rates, the decision to remove Covid reliefs for the sector means that City Pub Company’s business rates bills will rise by 30pc in the next three years.

‘I’m not sure I’ve ever felt this angry’

But the impact of Ms Reeves’s shake-up is not confined to large employers like Whitbread or City Pub either.

Maurice Abboudi, who runs Japanese takeaway chain K10 Restaurants in London, said his group will be paying an additional £40,000 in business rates from next year – taking its total rates bill to £180,000 on revenues of just £4m.

“The result is stagnation,” he says, adding that K10 Restaurants will be unable to expand because of the higher costs.

Roxanne Marjoram, from restaurants and pubs owner Gusto Pronto, also said her business is facing a £110,700 rates bill – a 39pc rise on last year.

“I am not sure I have ever felt this angry or so let down,” Marjoram added.

The Treasury says it is “protecting pubs, restaurants and cafes with the Budget’s £4.3bn support package – capping bill rises so a typical independent pub will pay around £4,800 less next year than they otherwise would have”.

And it is not only the high street where the Budget has caused ripples of anger.

According to Ryan, airports are also in the firing line. Heathrow and Gatwick expected to see their rates bills more than double within three years.

Heathrow’s bill will rise to £240m in three years’ time, while Gatwick’s bill is expected to surpass £80m by the 2028-29 financial year.

Manchester Airports Group on Monday said increases of more than 100pc in its business rates meant it would have to “look again at our plans to invest more than £2bn in our airports across the UK over the next five years”.

Yet, the business rates changes have not been bad news for all businesses.

Where there have been losers from Reeves’ shake-up, others have emerged as winners – although they have notably been keeping their heads down in the aftermath of the Budget.

Alongside museums and art galleries, creative sectors are looking at a boost from the changes.

Simon Berkley, a partner at Knight Frank says film and TV studios are “clear winners in the Budget”.

Not only did the so-called “rateable values” of film and TV studio sites go down by 27pc, but the Government also extended the previously awarded 40pc rates relief for the sector.

Berkley says it is worth remembering that this follows previous business rates increases, meaning that bills “remain significantly higher for the sector than they have been historically”.

Meanwhile, while frustration is rife across most retailers, there is one pocket where bosses are feeling quietly relieved.

After months of lobbying and warning over higher grocery bills, supermarket bosses appear set to escape the worst of the business rates rise.

Analysis by Bira suggests that a grocery superstore will pay around £70,000 less in business rates because the value of their property is not rising as sharply as smaller retailers.

One grocery insider said they were still “crunching the numbers”, but that they were unlikely to be paying more in business rates for large superstores.

For business chiefs like Jones from Philip Morris & Sons, it is this which is particularly “galling”.

“The Government implied that the big retailers were going to be paying a premium to subsidise smaller independents on the high street, and that’s simply not happening,” he says.

In his view, it simply does not feel like Labour is “levelling the playing field”.

“I guess you could say Reeves has caved to the big players and left the smaller ones very much stranded.”

data.title: US billionaire donates $6bn to fund ‘Trump accounts’ for children
data.description:
An American technology billionaire has pledged $6.25bn (£4.7bn) to help fund millions of child investment accounts set up by Donald Trump.

Michael Dell, the founder of PC-maker Dell, and his wife, Susan, have agreed to offer $250 to 25 million children, with the cash set to be paid into “Trump accounts” to invest in the US stock market.

The donation – which will be available to under-10s from families with a median income under $150,000 – is believed to be the single biggest philanthropic gift to American children.

However, it is also a sign of support for Mr Trump, whose administration unveiled the so-called Invest America programme earlier this year. This will see $1,000 paid into an investment account for every child born in 2025 or later.

Mr Dell’s pledge will mean an extra $250 deposited into the accounts of children who qualify.

Following the donation, Mr Trump said on social media: “Two great people. I love Dell!!!”

Mr Dell added: “We’ve seen what happens when a child gets even a small financial head start.

“We know that this program will give young Americans more than a savings account. It will give them momentum. It will give them confidence and opportunity.”

‘Make every new child a capitalist’

The savings accounts, which the US Treasury plans to launch next year, are intended to bolster financial literacy among children.

Republican senator Ted Cruz also told the Financial Times last year that the initiative would also “make every new child a capitalist”.

Families and employers will be able to deposit funds into the accounts, with children given the chance to withdraw the money once they turn 18.

Mr Dell, 60, founded his eponymous Dell Technologies in Texas in 1984, becoming the youngest chief executive of a Fortune 500 company at the time, aged 27.

He remains the majority owner of the company, which is also backed by the private equity firm Silver Lake.

The billionaire’s philanthropic fund, the Michael & Susan Dell Foundation, has donated $2.9bn since its inception in 1999.

However, their latest donation comes as a string of US technology billionaires queue up to donate to projects backed by Mr Trump.

Google, Microsoft, Meta and OpenAI all previously handed millions to Mr Trump’s inauguration fund, while the likes of Apple, Amazon and Palantir have all contributed funds to help build a new $300m ballroom at the White House.

Mr Trump passed the Invest America Act as part of the ‘big, beautiful bill’  this summer, intended to fund investment accounts for American children.

The act was introduced by Mr Cruz and backed by Brad Gerstner, a technology investor.

The White House is pushing ahead with Trump accounts even as the US state cuts back on funding aimed at reducing poverty in America, including cutting Medicaid and food stamps.

Critics have argued that the least well-off families will end up with less after the cutbacks.

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn a year to energy bills
data.description:
The troubled Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn annually to UK energy bills as soon as it’s switched on, official figures show.

The money will be taken from consumers and handed to the French owner EDF to subsidise operations, making it one of the UK’s most expensive sources of electricity.

A further £1bn will be added to bills by a separate nuclear levy, supporting construction of the Sizewell C nuclear power station in Suffolk, also led by EDF. Campaigners branded it a “nuclear tax on households”.

Details were revealed in documents released by the Treasury and the Office for Budget Responsibility in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

They describe how EDF will be entitled to claim the money under the “Contracts for Difference” subsidy system as soon as Hinkley C begins operations, probably in 2030.

The documents state: “In 2030-31, Contracts for Difference (CfDs) are expected to generate £4.6bn in government receipts, including £1bn to fund subsidy payments to the Hinkley Point C nuclear power plant for its first year of expected generation.”

The impact on bills is linked to a 2013 agreement reached between EDF and Sir Ed Davey, the then energy secretary. He guaranteed that EDF could charge £92.50 per megawatt hour (MWh) of power once Hinkley Point C came online. With inflation, this equates to £133 today and is expected to reach about £150 in 2030.

If the wholesale cost of electricity remains at its current level of about £80/MWh, then EDF can claim an extra £70 from consumers and businesses via CfDs.

From January, energy bills will also be hit by an entirely separate levy designed to support the construction of another nuclear power station at Sizewell in Suffolk.

The Regulated Asset Base levy will add £10 a year to power bills from 2026, raising £700m, but will roughly double by 2030, when it will need to raise £1.4bn a year for Sizewell.

Official modelling suggests Sizewell C’s final costs could reach £100bn.

Bills could rise even further once approval is given for another nuclear site on Anglesey in north Wales, where the UK’s first small modular reactors are planned.

‘We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax’

Alison Downes, of Stop Sizewell C, said nuclear power had proven to be among the most expensive ways of generating electricity.

She said: “The Government has a misguided belief that nuclear will be a cheap, ‘green’ solution to our energy needs, but the evidence shows the opposite – that costs of delivery and of dealing with nuclear waste – will continue to rise.

“We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax on households, given the acknowledged uncertainty about the projected costs of constructing Sizewell C.”

The surging cost of CfDs is part of a much broader increase in energy-related environmental levies. These add £14bn to energy bills at the moment, but this will rise to £19bn by the end of the decade, according to Treasury forecasts.

The CfD subsidy system, which also supports wind and solar, currently costs consumers and businesses £2.3bn a year, but those costs will hit £4.6bn by 2030-31.

Meanwhile, the Renewables Obligation, which funded the UK’s first wind farms, adds costs of £8bn a year.

The Capacity Market, which pays gas-fired power stations and hydroelectric facilities to be on standby in case wind and solar are hit by low wind and light levels, adds a further £1.6bn.

A government spokesman said the subsidies were necessary to unleash its planned “golden age of nuclear power”.

They said: “Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C will each deliver clean electricity for six million households for at least six decades. Sizewell C could save £2bn a year across the future low-carbon electricity system once operational, resulting in cheaper power for consumers.”

Hinkley C was initially forecast to cost £18bn, but spiralling construction costs have pushed the total bill to £46bn, equivalent to £700 per person in the UK.

EDF was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: This is the Chancellor’s biggest lie yet
data.description:
Of all the fake news that tumbled out of the Chancellor’s mouth on Budget day, the notion that this Government is about to restore some sense of normality to Britain’s broken energy system is perhaps the biggest lie of all.

Not that your typical family would know any better of course, which is precisely the point. To the vast majority of households, £150 off an annual energy bill is not to be sniffed at, especially during these straitened times.

The move helps to paint a picture of a Labour Government looking out for working people, not to mention honouring a key manifesto pledge to make energy more affordable.

Factor in a further £150 towards your energy costs through the Warm Home Discount for those on benefits and bills might start to look even more manageable for the poorest in the country.

Indeed, add the two together and it is the view of some Labour MPs that the Government will soon be able to claim that it has met its promise to cut energy bills by £300 – at least for the six million or so people that fall into this camp.

It truly is desperate stuff from a party heading for oblivion. The reality is that Labour deserves no applause whatsoever for this cynical attempt to pull the wool over our eyes.

The proposals laid out by Rachel Reeves at the despatch box amount to financial sleight of hand of the very worst – a cynical confidence trick played on unsuspecting voters without the knowledge to spot what is happening.

Yes, there’s a £150 reduction coming, but to pretend that this is some sort of permanent adjustment that means we’re heading to a world where it will be cheaper to heat our homes and turn on the lights is totally disingenuous.

What the Chancellor announced on Wednesday is nothing more than a short-term reprieve from an energy system that has become a dangerous mess and where bills are only going to get more prohibitively expensive.

What’s more, it is a situation that has been largely created by catastrophic policy decisions from a Government that has no idea what it’s doing. So for Labour to be asking everyone to be thankful for “bringing down energy bills” and easing the cost of living – as Reeves claimed straight-faced on Wednesday – really does take some beating.

The Treasury’s £150 saving is nothing more than a ruse designed to make people look the other way, while Labour piles on yet more levies and crushes domestic production with its decimation of the North Sea, sending bills further into the stratosphere in the process.

The basis for the £150 reduction is fairly straightforward once you lay it out. The Government is ditching the so-called energy company obligation, a scheme that forces energy suppliers to fund energy efficiency measures such as home insulation for low-income households. That will save a typical household £67 a year, experts say.

Plus, it was introduced under the Cameron Government, so Labour presumably thinks it gets bonus points for laying some of the current unaffordability at the door of the Opposition.

Then there’s a further £78 annual saving through the partial removal of the renewables obligation – effectively another green levy to pay for the construction of wind farms.

Yet the part that the Chancellor has shamefully chosen to be less forthcoming about is the long list of green levy increases barrelling down the track – increases that are bound to wipe out any savings over the long-term.

Still, at least the Office for Budget Responsibility – fresh from its attempt to torpedo the Budget entirely – is living up to its name and keeping count.

It notes that the cost of the capacity market – which pays for gas fired power stations, battery parks and hydro-electric plants to be on stand-by in the event that wind and solar plants fail – will jump from £1bn to about £4.5bn over the next five years.

As the OBR wonks point out: “Renewable energy sources ... have more variable energy generation compared to non-renewable sources.”

Similarly the costs of Contracts for Difference – yet more subsidies which fund wind and solar plants – will more than double from about £2bn this year to north of £4bn by 2030.

Then there’s the cost of financing Sizewell C in Suffolk, expected to spiral from £300m today to £1.4bn at the end of the decade, plus the Warm Home Discount that will add a further £1.1bn to annual bills.

Add in the estimated £150 rise in bills due to the cost of upgrading the national grid and the picture is much clearer: energy costs are set to go through the roof as a direct result of this Government’s insistence on going hell for leather with renewables.

Again, this is laid out in black and white by the OBR. It calculates that the cost of green taxes on energy bills will surge from £14bn this year to £19bn by 2030 under Reeves’s Budget proposals. Twenty years ago, receipts from environmental levies totalled just £300m.

As the OBR neatly puts it, “this reflects a shift away from the taxation of energy extraction to taxation on the consumption of energy”, so voters should be in no doubt about what is happening here.

It wouldn’t be quite so bad if Labour possessed the foresight to smooth the so-called energy transition with home-grown oil and gas reserves – but instead, the Government has embarked on a wilful destruction of the North Sea at the same time.

Faced with a series of anti-oil and gas policies, including the windfall tax, offshore operators are abandoning the region in their droves in what is set to trigger a near-collapse in tax receipts. Tax revenues from North Sea oil and gas will plummet ninefold from £2.7bn to just £300m between 2025 and 2030, the OBR says.

It also means the UK will become increasingly reliant on more costly imports of fossil fuels from the likes of Norway, Qatar and the US at a time when it already boasts the most expensive electricity prices in the world. The UK grid operator has warned that the amount of gas produced by the North Sea will decline by over 80pc this year alone.

The effect of all this then is to keep bills higher than they need to be at the same time as massively undermining energy security. How any of this makes sense is one of the great puzzles of our time. In the hands of Sir Keir Starmer, Reeves and Ed Miliband, this country is firmly on the path to self-destruction.

data.title: Chip giant caught in Dutch-Chinese row warns carmakers of factory shutdowns
data.description:
A major chip company that was seized by the Dutch government has warned of “imminent production stoppages” at carmakers amid a standoff with its rogue Chinese branch.

Nexperia, which produces semiconductors used across the car industry, said in an open letter to staff at its Chinese division that factories risked shutdown without a reopening of relations.

The Dutch business said it had been “compelled to communicate publicly” after its “repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue” were ignored.

The chipmaker has been at the centre of a geopolitical crisis after Dutch officials invoked a Cold War-era law to allow it to take effective control of the business. The Government justified the move by warning that the company’s Chinese owners, Wingtech, posed a threat to Europe’s chip supplies and accused them of governance failings.

The decision prompted Beijing to impose export controls on Nexperia’s chips, while staff at its Chinese business stopped following instructions from its European headquarters.

The blockade threatened to devastate Europe’s car industry and prompted The Hague to back down. The Dutch government revoked its order earlier this month after negotiations to diffuse the crisis.

Nexperia’s technology is used in dozens of electronic components that feature in modern cars, ranging from electronic window controls to windscreen wipers.

Wingtech’s shares in the business remain under the control of a court-appointed administrator in the Netherlands and its Chinese chief executive has also been suspended.

On Thursday, Nexperia claimed its Chinese branch had continued to ignore attempts to restart normal business despite the detente between Dutch and Chinese governments.

“Nexperia has made repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue with Nexperia’s entities in China by means of direct outreach via calls, emails and proposed meetings, various offers to collaborate on mutually beneficial solutions and even formal correspondence to demand performance of rights,” the company said.

“Regrettably, Nexperia did not receive any meaningful response. We urge you to respond to Nexperia’s outreaches and engage constructively and with a view to restoring the supply chain, as this is in the interest of all parties involved, including notably our customers.

“In light of this, we are compelled to communicate publicly to underscore the urgency.”

A Wingtech spokesman said Nexperia’s letter was “highly misleading” and said it had “repeatedly expressed our willingness to engage in constructive communication”.

The spokesman said: “We have also, on numerous occasions through multiple channels, reached out to Nexperia to pursue a negotiated solution.

“If the control dispute remains unresolved, numerous customers could face supply chain disruptions.”

Separately on Friday, Wingtech said it had filed an appeal against a court order stripping it of control of Nexperia in the Netherlands with the country’s supreme court.

data.title: Gulf royalty strikes deal to build thousands of London homes
data.description:
A Dubai-based property developer owned by Middle Eastern royalty has struck a deal to build thousands of homes alongside the River Thames.

Arada, co-founded by Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi of Sharjah, a city in the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia’s Prince Khaled bin Alwaleed bin Talal, has acquired an 80pc stake in Thameside West, a 47-acre development project in east London.

The scheme is set to deliver at least 5,000 homes near Royal Victoria Dock, and is estimated to be worth £2.5bn once completed.

It signals the growing influx of Middle Eastern money into London, particularly in the property sector.

Arada acquired the stake in Thameside West from London developer Keystone, paying an upfront fee of £225m alongside a pledge to invest a further £100m.

Keystone will own the remaining 20pc of the site, which is nearly twice the size of Hudson Yards in Manhattan, New York.

The acquisition is also one of the biggest land deals in London since 2020, giving the capital a much-needed boost after a recent slump in demand.

Just 5,933 new-build private homes were sold during the first three quarters of 2025, according to findings published by consultancy Molior London last month, with sales “weak across all local markets and at every price point”.

Dwindling sales have also led to a drop in construction, with activity hampered by high costs and uncertainty prompted by this month’s Budget.

Recent estimates show that just 15,000 to 20,000 private homes are to be under construction in London by the end of 2026.

‘Dubai model’

However, Ahmed Alkhoshaibi, the chief executive and co-founder of Arada, is confident that demand will recover.

“We will build and it will come,” he said. “That’s been the Dubai model. We will build even if there isn’t demand upfront.”

Mr Alkhoshaibi added that it is “the right time” to invest. He said: “It’s always the best time to buy when the price of the land is down as a developer.

“To create spaces that are on another level, you need to buy at the right price. Right now, London is down, but we’re confident it will be up sooner than later.”

It comes after Arada bought London housing developer Regal for £230m in September, with the aim of building 30,000 homes in the capital over the next three years.

During the 1990s, Prince Khaled was known for enjoying a lavish lifestyle, reportedly owning 200 luxury cars.

However, the guilt he experienced from taking part in a trophy hunt in South Africa in the latter part of the decade prompted him to adopt a more pared-back lifestyle – selling off his collection, becoming vegan and driving just one car, a Tesla.

His family also owns nearly 50pc of the Four Seasons international hotel brand, with the other half owned by Bill Gates.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Brewers push to increase real ale prices
data.description:
Brewers have said the price of a pint of real ale should rise, arguing that its cheaper cost at the bar is at odds with growing demand from Gen Z drinkers.

The 160-year-old Camerons Brewery said it was “pure madness” that real ale was treated as inferior to fashionable craft beers despite a revival among younger drinkers.

Craft keg beer costs up to £1.77 more for a pint than a traditional cask real ale, according to data from industry website the Morning Advertiser.

The Hartlepool-based Camerons Brewery told the trade journal: “It’s pure madness that the crafting and care required to produce and serve a quality pint of cask beer has found itself in a market where the price expectations are lower than keg.

“There is a growing young demographic interested in drinking cask, but it seems more to do with the experience than the price.”

Cask ale is typically much cheaper than lager or craft ale. The price differential has historically reflected lower demand, the extra cost of chilling and carbonating keg beers, and the shorter shelf life of cask ale, meaning pubs must sell it faster.

An annual survey of beer prices found that the average price of a pint of Doom Bar or Hobgoblin in the UK was £4.57, compared with £6.34 for BrewDog Punk IPA.

Sales of cask ale have fallen from £982m in 2023 to an estimated £947m in 2025, while its share of the beer market has dropped from 8.3pc to 7.7pc.

In the same time period craft beer sales have also fallen. Sales of stout, overseas lager and low-alcohol beers are the fastest growing parts of the market.

This comes despite significant increases in their price. The average cost of a pint of Guinness has risen to £5.56 – up more than £1 on last year – while Continental beers such as Peroni and Birra Moretti are more than £6 a pint.

However, cask ale is believed to be enjoying a revival among younger drinkers. One survey this year found that one in four Gen Z drinkers regularly order it at pubs, up 50pc on a year ago.

The Campaign for Real Ale (Camra) said that pints were in fact becoming too expensive for many drinkers thanks to taxes on beer itself and the cost of running pubs.

Ash Corbett-Collins, its chairman, said: “Cask beer is part of our heritage that goes hand-in-hand with the great British pub. Its quality should be kept in top notch so brewers and publicans can serve a viable, reasonably priced product to drinkers and gain a fair profit.

“Sadly, a trip down to your local is becoming unaffordable for many, and the rising cost of a pint has unfortunately become commonplace.

He added: “A reduced draught duty rate on pints of beer and cider in pubs would give breweries a chance to set lower prices on beer sold to pubs, which we hope would be carried to consumers at the bar. 

“But the Budget confirmed alcohol duty will go up across the board in February next year so consumers could see even more rises at the bar.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US streaming giants are taking cash-strapped Britons for granted
data.description:
If you are looking forward to watching A Merry Little Ex-Mas, starring Alicia Silverstone as a divorced woman trying to host a perfect family holiday before her ex-husband arrives with his new girlfriend; or My Secret Santa, about a single mother trying to get a job as a ski resort Father Christmas – and let’s be honest here, who could possibly want to miss that – then there is some bad news.

The Netflix subscription required to treat yourself to those films is more expensive than last year. Likewise, the Spotify sub to listen to the Jingle Bells playlist – or put All I Want For Christmas Is You on repeat – will cost more as well.

The streaming giants have discovered the secret to making lots of money. They have hooked us all on their services and so they push up the prices. 

Yet they can’t keep that up forever – because eventually the customers are going to get tired of it.

The days of cheap streaming are now long in the past. It emerged last week that Spotify is planning to raise its prices in the crucial US market early in the new year, after already raising the price in Britain, Switzerland and Australia.

What started as a $9.99 (£7.55) a month deal for listening to every piece of music ever made has already gone up to $11.99 and looks set to climb higher. It is not hard to understand why.

JP Morgan analysts estimate that even a $1-a-month increase in the US will add $500m a year to the company’s revenue. Goldman Sachs estimated in September that the music streamers, which have so far been relatively restrained compared with television, would increase prices every 12 to 24 months from now on.

Likewise, Netflix increased its standard monthly package by $2.50 in the US this year and in the UK by £2 a month. It has also started showing ads to boost revenues and you have to pay extra to skip them. Apple TV put up its prices by 30pc in the summer and Disney+ by $3 a month in October.

In the background, the streaming services are consolidating.

ITV could get sold to Sky, now owned by the American giant Comcast – and that would include its streaming service as well as its terrestrial channel. Paramount Global is in the process of being sold to David Ellison’s emerging Skydance conglomerate, while there are reports of a mega-merger between Paramount and Warner Bros to create a genuine rival to Netflix.

There are going to be fewer streaming services but each one will have a lock on its market and may well feel it can keep on pushing up prices.

Of course, we can all understand that is very good for business. Streaming is a great industry if you get it right. True, Spotify shares some of its revenues with the record labels – and even occasionally with the artists – and the movie deals can be complex.

But as a rule, if you can make a price increase stick, the extra revenues drop straight down to the bottom line.

Spotify shares are up 30pc this year, with the increase driven as much by the prospect of price rises as by new subscribers. Netflix shares are up by 20pc since the start of the year, an impressive result for a fairly mature business. All the evidence suggests those price rises can be made to stick. We are hooked on the service and it will take a lot to persuade anyone to give it up now.

The trouble is, there are two big problems with the attempt to constantly squeeze more and more money out of a captive audience. First, consumers are becoming exhausted.

Sure, at first an extra pound or dollar does not matter very much. The subscription typically gets taken straight out of your bank account and it might be a while before you even notice.

Yet that will not be true forever. With a stagnant economy in the UK, with inflation rising and with taxes going up all the time, at some point people will turn off in droves. Once they have gone, it will be very hard to get them back again.

Next, it opens up the market for lower-cost competitors. In television, the Fox-owned Tubi offers ad-supported broadcasting. YouTube is an alternative to Spotify for anyone who gets tired of the price rises.

There are already platforms that allow artists to sell their music directly to their fans and they may grow in popularity, while sports teams may find it makes more sense financially to cut out the middlemen.

The important point is this. Once your TV or speakers are hooked up to the internet, there are lots of different ways of receiving content and entrepreneurs will constantly experiment with new ways of making that work.

The more expensive the existing giants become, the more tempting some of those rivals will look – and once they get started, they may be able to expand very quickly.

In reality, the streamers are taking the market for granted. They are hoping that a handful of giants will have such a grip on consumers that they can do whatever they want. Netflix is certainly already in that position and so is Amazon’s Prime service, and perhaps Disney as well. So are Spotify and Apple Music.

Their competitors have either fallen by the wayside or else they are furiously merging with one another so that they can join the elite before it is too late. Investors are betting that they can keep on effortlessly pushing up the cost of a subscription and make more and more money. Perhaps they can.

Yet that is one of the riskiest business strategies of all time. Eventually, the customer will get fed up and someone will work out how to offer a cheaper service – and then the streaming giants will find that price does matter after all.

data.title: Pay attention to Miss Universe – it’s where the real action is
data.description:
It’s odd to reflect it was perfectly normal for Britons in the 1970s to sit round the TV watching beauty pageants presented by avuncular chaps like Michael Aspel. Little girls like me wondered if our short, plump legs would one day magically transform into the coltish limbs of a glamazon.

Miss Universe never took off here to the extent as Miss World, which was screened on ITV. On top of that, my friends and I found the title misleading. Where was Miss Mars and bright green beauties with tentacles? Before I could raise a petition, BBC1’s controller Michael Grade took beauty shows off the corporation’s screens in 1985. ITV eventually dropped Miss World in 1988.

This, however, may have been a mistake, as few things entertain like watching a beauty contest descend into taloned flounce-outs and hair-tugging. This year’s Miss Universe contest in Thailand has had drama on a par with Dynasty in its heyday.

For those who missed it, here’s a swift canter through the shenanigans, which included two judges resigning before events had properly got going. French-Lebanese composer Omar Harfouch expressed “profound confusion and concern” on discovering that 30 finalists appeared to have been preselected – from 136 contestants – without the official judges being consulted. The claim was denied by organisers, but didn’t stop former French footballer Claude Makélélé stepping down too for “personal reasons”. Then Miss Jamaica fell and was stretchered off to hospital, while Miss Israel was accused of giving “side-eye” to Miss Palestine, though this was probably due to an unfortunate camera angle.

These were just the amuse-bouches before Thai media personality Nawat Itsaragrisil (newly-appointed executive director of Miss Universe) was caught on camera berating Miss Mexico, Fatima Bosch, for “disrespecting” the host country by not feverishly posting about the contest on social media.

Bosch says Nawat called her a “dumbhead”, he protested he merely said she’d “done damage”. Either way, Bosch wasn’t having any of it and stalked out followed by a posse of co-contestants, while Nowat yelled, “Stop! Stop! Security!” Bosch swiftly garnered support from MUA (Miss Universe Association) President Raúl Rocha, who also hails from Mexico. He denounced Nawat’s “malicious” behaviour as intimidating “a defenceless woman”.

Bosch appeared far from cowed. She proceeded to carry off Darth Vader’s Galactic Miss Universe title, spending the next few days issuing statements about female empowerment: “I came here to be a voice for all the women and all the girls who fight for causes.” Rocha backed her up, saying Miss Universe “is an empowerment platform for women”.

His feminist virtue-signalling might have been more impactful if Mexican news outlet El Reforma hadn’t reported that a warrant had been issued against Rocha for trafficking drugs, arms and fuel between Mexico and Guatemala. It was enough to make you yearn for the good old days when Donald Trump owned the franchise and introduced beauty queens to the likes of Jeffrey Epstein and… Oh, hang on a moment.

The fact is that beauty contests have long felt like a way for suspect businessmen to meet gorgeous young women escaping ordinary backgrounds. You don’t get the daughters of dukes entering these pageants, no matter how goddess-like.

But the debacle also reminds us that Miss Universe is alive and thriving, despite disappearing from terrestrial TV in Britain. In fact, the 74th staging of Miss Universe was the most-watched Spanish language programme of 2025, viewed by an “unprecedented” 2.6 billion people across social media in countries like (surprise, surprise) Mexico, the Philippines, Puerto Rico and Venezuela.

The last country even has its own “beauty factory” programme to prepare contenders for pageant life, resulting in more “Big Four” titles (Miss Universe, Miss World, Miss International and Miss Earth) than any other country.

It’s easy to dismiss Miss Universe, but it’s big, big business and tells us much about geopolitics – rather like our very own Eurovision Song Contest. South American nations favour Latin beauty queens in much the same way the Baltic States vote for one another in Eurovision, then award “nil points” to Britain.

Miss Africa and Oceana, Olivia Yacé, resigned post-competition after protesting that African women weren’t being “heard”. Meanwhile, the all-conquering Bosch couldn’t stop issuing girl-power statements like: “When one woman raises her voice, we all raise our voices.” Try telling that to the women of Afghanistan.

But the platitudes are designed to distract us from the men pulling the levers – deciding who lands the real prize of a TV goldmine.

data.title: Investment tycoon restoring Bishop Auckland Castle slashes jobs
data.description:
An investment tycoon helping to fund the restoration of Bishop Auckland Castle has cut a fifth of jobs at his firm after profits slumped.

Ruffer, which was founded in 1994 by Jonathan Ruffer, said that it had cut 72 roles in the year to the end of March, according to accounts filed at Companies House.

The 22pc reduction in its total headcount “significantly” reduced costs and improved resilience, bosses said.

Operating profits dropped by 21pc to £93.9m over the period, while assets under management fell by 15pc to £19.5bn.

Mr Ruffer said in his annual update earlier this year that his firm had “failed to meet its objectives” for the second year in a row.

The boutique’s funds were wrong-footed by its mistaken belief that the US stock market would fall and that the Japanese yen would strengthen against the dollar, he said.

Addressing the US update, Mr Ruffer wrote: “The nature of bubble valuations is that they somehow offer subliminal validation on the journey towards the moon.”

The downturn comes as the 74-year-old prepares to retire as chairman of his eponymous fund manager by the end of 2025 after more than three decades at the business. Henry Maxey, the company’s co-chief investment officer, will take over as chairman at the start of next year.

Mr Ruffer will instead focus on charitable work, which is focused on regenerating the market town of Bishop Auckland, County Durham, near where he grew up.

A devout evangelical Christian, Mr Ruffer helped establish the Auckland Castle Trust, which went on to renovate what has been the home of the Bishop of Durham since the 14th century and preserve paintings hung in the building.

He spent £15m to purchase a series of historic works by Spanish artist Francisco de Zurbarán that were due to be sold to an unnamed Russian oligarch. The paintings had been hanging in the castle for 250 years.

The amount of profit available to Ruffer’s 48 partners for the year fell to £71m, down from £89m in the previous year. The total payout is equivalent to £1.5m for each partner, although some are likely to have earned more than others.

According to the company’s update in October, the investment firm’s flagship total return fund was up by 10.7pc so far this year.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”
